Ayahs

حمٓ (١)
Ha, Meem. (1)
تَنزِيلُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْحَكِيمِ (٢)
The [gradual] sending down of the Book is from Allah, the All-mighty, the All-wise. (2)
مَا خَلَقْنَا ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَآ إِلَّا بِٱلْحَقِّ وَأَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى ۚ وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ عَمَّآ أُنذِرُوا۟ مُعْرِضُونَ (٣)
We did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them except with consummate wisdom and for a specified term. Yet the faithless are disregardful of what they are warned. (3)

Contents of Surah al-Ahqaf

This Surah is among the Meccan Surahs (although a group of commentators have confirmed the revelation of some of its verses in Medina, which we will discuss in the explanation of those verses). Given the conditions of the time and place of its revelation—which was a period of struggle against polytheism and a call to Monotheism (Tawhid), the Resurrection (Ma'ad), and the foundational issues of Islam—it speaks within these same axes. In a concise summary, it can be said that this Surah pursues the following objectives:

  1. Expressing the greatness of the Quran.

  2. A decisive struggle against all forms of polytheism and idol-worship.

  3. Orienting the people regarding the issue of the Resurrection and the Court of Justice of the Lord.

  4. Meanwhile, as a warning to the polytheists and criminals, it recounts a portion of the story of the people of 'Ad, who inhabited the land of "Ahqaf" (the name of the Surah is also derived from this).

  5. Referring to the expansion and universality of the Prophet of Islam's invitation, to the extent that it includes beings other than humans, namely the tribe of Jinn.

  6. Encouraging the believers and warning the disbelievers, and establishing the principles of fear and hope.

  7. Inviting the Prophet of Islam to even greater patience and steadfastness, and to follow the tradition of the great previous Prophets.

The Virtue of this Surah

In a Hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله regarding the virtue of this Surah, it is stated:

Tafsir Nemouneh; Vol. 21; p. 295

من قرأ سورة الاحقاف اعطى من الاجر بعدد كل رمل فى الدنيا عشر حسنات، و محى عنه عشر سيئات، و رفع له عشر درجات:

"Whoever recites Surah al-Ahqaf, for every grain of sand that exists in this world, ten good deeds shall be granted to him, ten sins shall be erased, and ten ranks shall be added to his degrees." ^1 Since "Ahqaf" is the plural of "Hiqf" (rhyming with Rizq), meaning shifting sands that accumulate in deserts in long, winding shapes due to the blowing of the wind—and the land of the people of 'Ad was called "Ahqaf" because it was a sandy region of this nature—the expression in the above Hadith alludes to this meaning.

It is evident that such rewards and degrees are not merely for the recitation of words, but rather for a constructive and awakening recitation on the path of faith and piety; and the content of Surah al-Ahqaf truly possesses such an effect if a person is a seeker of truth and ready for action.

In another Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is stated:

من قرأ كل ليلة او كل جمعة سورة الاحقاف لم يصبه اللَّه عز و جل بروعة فى الحياة الدنيا، و آمنه من فزع يوم القيامة ان شاء اللَّه:

"Whoever recites Surah al-Ahqaf every night or every Friday, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, will remove the terrors of the worldly life from him, and will keep him safe from the dread of the Day of Resurrection, if Allah wills." [^2]

[^2]: Tafsir "Majma' al-Bayan" and "Nur al-Thaqalayn," beginning of Surah al-Ahqaf.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ‌

حم (1) تَنْزِيلُ الْكِتابِ مِنَ اللَّهِ الْعَزِيزِ الْحَكِيمِ (2) ما خَلَقْنَا السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضَ وَ ما بَيْنَهُما إِلاَّ بِالْحَقِّ وَ أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى وَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا عَمَّا أُنْذِرُوا مُعْرِضُونَ (3)

Commentary

The Creation of This World is Based on Truth

This Surah is the last of the seven Surahs beginning with "Ha Mim," which are collectively called the "Hawamim."

Regarding the interpretation of the "Disjointed Letters" (al-Huruf al-Muqatta'ah) in general, and "Ha Mim" in particular, we have provided extensive material at the beginning of Surahs al-Baqarah, Al-Imran, al-A'raf, and the previous "Ha Mim" Surahs, which does not need to be repeated.

Suffice it to say that these soul-stirring, movement-inspiring, and content-rich verses of the Quran are composed of simple letters of the alphabet—from Ha and Mim and the like—and it is enough for the greatness of Allah that He has brought forth such a magnificent composition from such simple individual elements, which, if one were to reflect upon its secrets until the Day of Resurrection, there would still be much left unsaid.

And perhaps for this reason, He immediately adds: "The revelation of the Book is from Allah, the All-Mighty, the All-Wise" (تَنْزِيلُ الْكِتابِ مِنَ اللَّهِ الْعَزِيزِ الْحَكِيمِ). This is the same expression that appears at the beginning of three of the "Ha Mim" Surahs (Ghafir/Mu'min, al-Jathiyah, and al-Ahqaf).

It is certain that an invincible power and infinite wisdom are required to reveal such a Book.

Then, moving from the "Written Book" (Kitab al-Tadwin) to the "Book of Creation" (Kitab al-Takwin), He speaks of the greatness and truthfulness of the heavens and the earth, saying: "We did not create the heavens and the earth and what is between them except with truth" (ما خَلَقْنَا السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضَ وَ ما بَيْنَهُما إِلَّا بِالْحَقِ). Neither in His Heavenly Book is there a word contrary to the truth, nor in the entirety of His world of creation is anything found to be disproportionate or contrary to the truth; everything is balanced, everything is measured, and everything is accompanied by and coupled with the truth.

However, just as this creation had a beginning, it also has an end, and therefore in the continuation of the verse He adds: "and for a specified term" (وَ أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى).

With its arrival, this world will perish. And since this world is coupled with truth and possesses a purpose, naturally there must exist another world following it where the results of deeds are examined. Therefore, the truthfulness of this world is itself a proof for the existence of the Resurrection; otherwise, it would be hollow and futile, and coupled with much injustice and oppression.

Yet, despite the fact that the Quran is truth and the creation of the world is also truth, "the stubborn disbelievers turn away from that of which they are warned" (وَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا عَمَّا أُنْذِرُوا مُعْرِضُونَ).

On one hand, the verses of the Quran continuously warn and frighten them that a great Court lies ahead, and on the other hand, the world of creation with its specific systems also warns that there is an accounting involved. But these heedless, unaware people pay attention to neither this nor that.

The expression "mu'ridun" (those who turn away) from the root "i'rad" indicates that if they were to face and encounter the signs of creation and revelation, they would perceive the realities; however, they turn their faces away and flee from the truth, lest it create a change in the course of their traditions, imaginations, and whims.

Ayahs

قُلْ أَرَءَيْتُم مَّا تَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ أَرُونِى مَاذَا خَلَقُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ أَمْ لَهُمْ شِرْكٌ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ ۖ ٱئْتُونِى بِكِتَـٰبٍ مِّن قَبْلِ هَـٰذَآ أَوْ أَثَـٰرَةٍ مِّنْ عِلْمٍ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٤)
Say, ‘Tell me about those you invoke besides Allah. Show me what [part] of the earth have they created. Do they have any share in the heavens? Bring me a scripture [revealed] before this, or some vestige of [divine] knowledge, if you are truthful.’ (4)
وَمَنْ أَضَلُّ مِمَّن يَدْعُوا۟ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مَن لَّا يَسْتَجِيبُ لَهُۥٓ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ وَهُمْ عَن دُعَآئِهِمْ غَـٰفِلُونَ (٥)
Who is more astray than him who invokes besides Allah such [entities] as would not respond to him until the Day of Resurrection, and who are oblivious of their invocation? (5)
وَإِذَا حُشِرَ ٱلنَّاسُ كَانُوا۟ لَهُمْ أَعْدَآءً وَكَانُوا۟ بِعِبَادَتِهِمْ كَـٰفِرِينَ (٦)
When mankind are mustered [on Judgement’s Day] they will be their enemies, and they will disavow their worship. (6)

Commentary

The Most Astray of People?

In the preceding verses, the discussion centered on the creation of the heavens and the earth## Commentary

The Most Astray of People?

In the preceding verses, the discussion centered on the creation of the heavens and the earth, all of which originate from Allah, the Almighty, the All-Wise. The necessary implication of this statement is that there is no deity in the universe except Him, for only He who is the Creator and Disposer of the universe is worthy of worship, and these two attributes are combined in His Pure Essence.

To complete this discussion, the verses under consideration address the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله, saying: "Say to these polytheists: 'Tell me, show me those deities you worship besides Allah; what part of the earth have they created?'" (قُلْ أَرَأَيْتُمْ مَا تَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ أَرُونِي مَاذَا خَلَقُوا مِنَ الْأَرْضِ).

"Or do they have a share in the creation of the heavens, or their ownership and governance?" (أَمْ لَهُمْ شِرْكٌ فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ).

When you admit that the idols have no involvement in the creation of earthly beings, nor in the creation of the sun, moon, stars, and the celestial beings, and you yourselves explicitly state that "the Creator of all these is Allah," [1] then how is it that to solve your problems or seek blessings, you cling to "idols"—these useless entities devoid of intellect and perception?! And if, hypothetically, you claim they had a share in the matter of creation, then "Bring me a book from the previous heavenly scriptures that confirms your statement, or any remnant of knowledge from past scholars that testifies to this meaning, if you are truthful" (ائْتُونِي بِكِتابٍ مِنْ قَبْلِ هذا أَوْ أَثارَةٍ مِنْ عِلْمٍ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ).

[^1]: This meaning appears in four verses of the Quran; for further explanation on this matter, please refer to the commentary on verse 25 of Surah Az-Zukhruf in this same volume of Tafsir Nemouneh.

In short: evidence is either traditional (naqli) through heavenly revelation, or intellectual and logical, or the testimony and witness of scholars. Since you, in your claims regarding idols, neither rely on revelation and a heavenly book, nor can you prove their partnership in the creation of the heavens and the earth to use this intellectual proof for their divinity, nor does any remnant of the knowledge of the ancients support your claim, it becomes clear that your religion and path are nothing but a handful of ugly superstitions and false imaginings.

Therefore, the phrase "أَرُونِي ما ذا خَلَقُوا مِنَ الْأَرْضِ..." (show me what they have created of the earth...) refers to intellectual proof, and the phrase "ائْتُونِي بِكِتابٍ مِنْ قَبْلِ هذا" (bring me a book before this) refers to heavenly revelation, and the expression "أَثارَةٍ مِنْ عِلْمٍ" (a remnant of knowledge) refers to the tradition of past Prophets and their successors, or the works of previous scholars. [1] Lexicographers and commentators have mentioned several meanings for "atharah" (on the pattern of halawah): "the remainder of something," "narration," and "sign," but it appears that all return to one meaning, which is the trace or effect that remains of something and serves as evidence for its existence.

A similar dialogue, reprimand, and trial of the idol-worshippers appears in verse 40 of Surah Fatir, where it says: "Say, 'Have you considered your "partners" whom you invoke besides Allah? Show me what they have created of the earth, or have they a partnership in the heavens? Or have We given them a book so they are [standing] on evidence therefrom? Rather, the wrongdoers promise one another nothing but delusion.'"

It is noteworthy that regarding the earth, it says: "ما ذا خَلَقُوا مِنَ الْأَرْضِ" (what part of the earth have they created?), and regarding the heavens, it says: "أَمْ لَهُمْ شِرْكٌ فِي السَّماواتِ" (or do they have a share in the heavens?), meaning in both places the talk is of partnership, because shirk (polytheism) in worship must stem from shirk in creation and governance.

Here, the question arises: if the polytheists usually considered the matter of creation to belong exclusively to Allah, what is the purpose of demanding one of these three types of evidence from them?

In response, it can be said that this demand is directed at a small group who were likely among the idol-worshippers and considered the idols as partners in creation, or that the matter is presented hypothetically—meaning, if you were to even think of such a claim regarding the idols' partnership in the creation of the world, you have no intellectual, traditional, or rational evidence for your claim.

[^1]: In a hadith narrated in Usul al-Kafi from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read regarding the interpretation of the phrase "أَوْ أَثارَةٍ مِنْ عِلْمٍ": "It refers to the knowledge of the successors (awsiya) of the Prophets" (Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 9).

Then, to describe the depth of the misguidance of these polytheists, it adds: "And who is more astray than he who invokes besides Allah those who will not respond to him until the Day of Resurrection?" (وَ مَنْ أَضَلُّ مِمَّنْ يَدْعُوا مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ مَنْ لا يَسْتَجِيبُ لَهُ إِلى‌ يَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ). Not only do they not respond to them, they do not hear their words at all "and they are unaware of their invocation!" (وَ هُمْ عَنْ دُعائِهِمْ غافِلُونَ).

Some commentators have considered the referent of the pronoun in this verse to be inanimate idols, fitting the fact that most of the deities of the Arab polytheists were these idols. Others believe it refers to angels and humans who were made objects of worship, as those who worshipped angels and jinn were not few among the Arabs; the various expressions in this verse that are suitable for rational beings (dhawi al-uqul) support this meaning.

However, there is no obstacle to interpreting the verse in its broad sense, so that all these deities—whether inanimate or living, rational or non-rational—are included in the concept of the verse, and the expressions suitable for rational beings are used by way of "taghlib" (predominance).

The statement that they will not respond to them until the Day of Resurrection does not mean they will respond to them on the Day of Resurrection—as some have imagined—but rather it is a common expression used for eternal negation. For example, we say: "If you insist to so-and-so until the Day of Resurrection, he will not give you a loan," meaning such a thing will never happen, not that he will fulfill your request on the Day of Resurrection.

The reason for this is also clear, because any kind of activity, effort, struggle, and responding to a call is useful in this world; when the world ends, the basis for all these matters is completely removed.

And even more tragic: "And when the people are gathered [on the Day of Resurrection], they [the deities] will be enemies to them and will be deniers of their worship!" (وَ إِذا حُشِرَ النَّاسُ كانُوا لَهُمْ أَعْداءً وَ كانُوا بِعِبادَتِهِمْ كافِرِينَ). Deities that possess intellect will officially rise in enmity; Prophet Jesus عليه السلام will disassociate himself from his worshippers, and the angels will also seek acquittal, even the devils and jinn will express their loathing. As for those that were without intellect, Allah will grant them life and intellect so they may speak and express their levels of enmity and hatred toward these worshippers.

Similar meanings appear in other verses of the Quran, including verse 14 of Surah Fatir, addressing the polytheists: "If you invoke them, they do not hear your supplication; and if they heard, they would not respond to you. And on the Day of Resurrection they will deny your association. And none can inform you like [one] Acquainted [with all matters]."

Here too, all the issues mentioned in the verses under discussion are repeated with a slight difference.

But how do the deities deny the worship of the worshippers when there is no room for denial?

It might be an indication that they were, in reality, worshipping their own carnal desires, not the deities, since the source of "idol-worship" is "desire-worship."

It is also noteworthy that the enmity and hostility of the deities toward the worshippers on the Day of Resurrection is not something emphasized only here. In verse 25 of Surah al-Ankabut, from the words of Abraham عليه السلام, the hero of idol-breaking, we also read: "And [Abraham] said, 'You have only taken other than Allah idols as [a bond of] affection among you in worldly life. Then on the Day of Resurrection you will deny one another and curse one another.'"

In verse 82 of Surah Maryam, it is also stated: "Nay! They will deny their worship and will be against them as opponents."

Ayahs

وَإِذَا تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَايَـٰتُنَا بَيِّنَـٰتٍ قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لِلْحَقِّ لَمَّا جَآءَهُمْ هَـٰذَا سِحْرٌ مُّبِينٌ (٧)
When Our manifest signs are recited to them, the faithless say of the truth when it comes to them: ‘This is plain magic.’ (7)
أَمْ يَقُولُونَ ٱفْتَرَىٰهُ ۖ قُلْ إِنِ ٱفْتَرَيْتُهُۥ فَلَا تَمْلِكُونَ لِى مِنَ ٱللَّهِ شَيْـًٔا ۖ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا تُفِيضُونَ فِيهِ ۖ كَفَىٰ بِهِۦ شَهِيدًۢا بَيْنِى وَبَيْنَكُمْ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلْغَفُورُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (٨)
Or they say, ‘He has fabricated it.’ Say, ‘Should I have fabricated it, you would not avail me anything against Allah. He best knows what you gossip concerning it. He suffices as a witness between me and you, and He is the All-forgiving, the All-merciful.’ (8)
قُلْ مَا كُنتُ بِدْعًا مِّنَ ٱلرُّسُلِ وَمَآ أَدْرِى مَا يُفْعَلُ بِى وَلَا بِكُمْ ۖ إِنْ أَتَّبِعُ إِلَّا مَا يُوحَىٰٓ إِلَىَّ وَمَآ أَنَا۠ إِلَّا نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ (٩)
Say, ‘I am not a novelty among the apostles, nor do I know what will be done with me, or with you. I just follow whatever is revealed to me, and I am just a manifest warner.’ (9)
قُلْ أَرَءَيْتُمْ إِن كَانَ مِنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ وَكَفَرْتُم بِهِۦ وَشَهِدَ شَاهِدٌ مِّنۢ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ عَلَىٰ مِثْلِهِۦ فَـَٔامَنَ وَٱسْتَكْبَرْتُمْ ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (١٠)
Say, ‘Tell me, if it is from Allah and you disbelieve in it, and a witness from the Children of Israel has testified to its like and believed [in it], while you are disdainful [of it]?’ Indeed Allah does not guide the wrongdoing lot. (10)

Commentary

Say, "I Am Not a Novel Messenger"

These verses continue to discuss the state of the polytheists and point to how they encountered the divine verses, saying: "And when Our clear verses are recited to them, those who disbelieve say of the truth when it has come to them, 'This is obvious magic'" (وَ إِذا تُتْلى‌ عَلَيْهِمْ آياتُنا بَيِّناتٍ قالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لِلْحَقِّ لَمَّا جاءَهُمْ هذا سِحْرٌ مُبِينٌ‌).

On one hand, they cannot deny the rapid, deep influence and the extraordinary attraction of the Quran within the depths of hearts; on the other hand, they are unwilling to bow before its truth and greatness. Therefore, they present this influence through a deviant interpretation as "obvious magic," which is itself a clear implicit admission of the Quran's extraordinary effect on human hearts! Thus, "the truth" (al-haqq) in the above verse refers to those very "verses of the Quran," although some have interpreted it to mean "prophethood," "Islam," or "other miracles of the Prophet" صلى الله عليه وآله. However, considering the beginning of the verse, the first interpretation seems more appropriate.

But they do not stop at this accusation; they go further and explicitly "say: He has fabricated it against Allah" (أَمْ يَقُولُونَ افْتَراهُ‌). Here, Allah commands His Prophet to answer them with clear reasoning, saying: "Say: If I have fabricated it, then you have no power to protect me from Allah at all" (قُلْ إِنِ افْتَرَيْتُهُ فَلا تَمْلِكُونَ لِي مِنَ اللَّهِ شَيْئاً) [1]. How could Allah allow these "clear verses" and this eternal miracle to appear at the hands of a liar?! This is far from the wisdom and grace of Allah.

As stated in verses 44 to 47 of Surah Al-Haqqah: وَ لَوْ تَقَوَّلَ عَلَيْنا بَعْضَ الْأَقاوِيلِ لَأَخَذْنا مِنْهُ بِالْيَمِينِ ثُمَّ لَقَطَعْنا مِنْهُ الْوَتِينَ فَما مِنْكُمْ مِنْ أَحَدٍ عَنْهُ حاجِزِينَ‌ "And if he (the Prophet of Islam) had made up any false sayings against Us, We would certainly have seized him by the right hand, then We would certainly have cut off his aorta, and not one of you could have withheld Us from him."

Therefore, how could I commit such a dangerous act for your sake? And how can you believe that I would speak such a lie and yet Allah would leave me alive and place great miracles at my disposal?

Then, as a threat, He adds: "But Allah knows best what you are engaged in" and He will punish you severely in due time (هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِما تُفِيضُونَ فِيهِ‌). Yes, He knows all these false attributions you make against me, how you have risen against His messenger, and how you prevent people from believing in the truth through your propaganda.

And in the next phrase, as a further emphasis combined with a polite tone, He adds:

"Sufficient is He as a witness between me and you" (كَفى‌ بِهِ شَهِيداً بَيْنِي وَ بَيْنَكُمْ‌).

He knows the truth of my call and my efforts in conveying the message, and He also sees your lies, fabrications, and obstructions; and this is sufficient for me and for you.

And to show them the way of return, He adds at the end of the verse:

"And He is the Forgiving, the Merciful" (وَ هُوَ الْغَفُورُ الرَّحِيمُ‌).

He forgives those who repent and includes them in His vast mercy.

In the next verse, He adds: "Say: I am not a novel messenger (unlike other messengers)" (قُلْ ما كُنْتُ بِدْعاً مِنَ الرُّسُلِ‌). "And I do not know what Allah will do with me, nor with you" (وَ ما أَدْرِي ما يُفْعَلُ بِي وَ لا بِكُمْ‌).

"I only follow what is revealed to me" (إِنْ أَتَّبِعُ إِلَّا ما يُوحى‌ إِلَيَ‌). "And I am not but a clear warner" (وَ ما أَنَا إِلَّا نَذِيرٌ مُبِينٌ‌).

These short and meaningful sentences are a response to many of the polytheists' objections. For instance, they sometimes expressed wonder at the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله mission, questioning how a human being could connect with God.

Sometimes they said: Why does he eat food and walk in the marketplaces? Sometimes they demanded strange and exotic miracles, each making a different request. Sometimes they expected the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to be a center of the knowledge of the unseen (Ghayb) and to foretell all future events for them. And finally, they sometimes wondered why he called to monotheism and the oneness of the Deity.

This verse is a summary response to all these discussions and excuses. It says: I am not the first messenger to call to monotheism; many messengers came before me, all of whom were human, wore clothes, and ate food. None of them claimed absolute knowledge of the unseen; rather, they said: We know of the unseen only what Allah has taught us. And none of them surrendered to "demanded miracles" and the whimsical suggestions of the people.

This is so everyone knows that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is also a servant among the servants of Allah; his knowledge and power are limited to what Allah wills. Absolute knowledge and power belong only to the Lord. These are realities that people must know to end their misplaced objections.

All of this follows the discussion in the previous verses where they sometimes accused the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله of magic and sometimes of fabrication. The source of these false attributions was those very misconceptions that were answered by this verse.

From here, it becomes clear that the content of this verse does not contradict other verses showing that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله had knowledge of the unseen—such as what is mentioned in Surah Al-Fath regarding the conquest of Mecca and entering Masjid al-Haram (Surah Al-Fath, verse 27), or what is mentioned regarding Christ عليه السلام who said: أُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِما تَأْكُلُونَ وَ ما تَدَّخِرُونَ فِي بُيُوتِكُمْ‌ "I inform you of what you eat and what you store in your houses" (Al-Imran, 44) and the like. This is because the verse under discussion denies "absolute knowledge of the unseen," not "any knowledge of the unseen." In other words, this verse denies independent knowledge of the unseen, while those verses speak of knowledge of the unseen by virtue of divine teaching.

The evidence for this is verses 26 and 27 of Surah Al-Jinn: عالِمُ الْغَيْبِ فَلا يُظْهِرُ عَلى‌ غَيْبِهِ أَحَداً إِلَّا مَنِ ارْتَضى‌ مِنْ رَسُولٍ‌ "He is the Knower of the unseen, and He does not disclose His unseen to anyone, except to a messenger whom He has chosen."

Some commentators have provided a "reason for revelation" (Shan-e Nuzul) for the verse under discussion, stating: When the pressure of difficulties increased upon the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله companions in Mecca, he saw in a dream that he would migrate to a land with palm groves, trees, and abundant water. He told this to his companions; they were all happy and thought that relief from the polytheists' harassment would soon come. They waited for a while but saw no sign of it. They said, "O Messenger of Allah! We have not seen what you described. When will we migrate to that land you saw in the dream?" The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله remained silent, and at that moment, the above verse was revealed: وَ ما أَدْرِي ما يُفْعَلُ بِي وَ لا بِكُمْ‌ "I do not know what Allah will do with me or with you" [2]. However, this reason for revelation seems unlikely because the audience in these verses is the Prophet's enemies, not his friends. But it might be a case of "application" (tatbiq), meaning that when this question was raised by his friends, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله cited this verse to answer them.

In the last verse under discussion, to complete what was mentioned in the previous verses, He adds: "Say: Tell me, if this Quran is from Allah and you disbelieve in it, while a witness from the Children of Israel has testified to the like of it and believed, while you were arrogant (and did not submit), who would be more astray than you?! Indeed, Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people" (قُلْ أَ رَأَيْتُمْ إِنْ كانَ مِنْ عِنْدِ اللَّهِ وَ كَفَرْتُمْ بِهِ وَ شَهِدَ شاهِدٌ مِنْ بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ عَلى‌ مِثْلِهِ فَآمَنَ وَ اسْتَكْبَرْتُمْ إِنَّ اللَّهَ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الظَّالِمِينَ‌) [3]. There is debate among commentators as to who this witness from the Children of Israel was who testified to the truth of the Holy Quran.

Some have said it refers to Moses bin Imran, who in his own time gave news of the appearance of the Prophet of Islam and his signs. However, this possibility is inconsistent with the phrase "and he believed while you were arrogant," which indicates that this witness from the Children of Israel believed in the Prophet of Islam while the polytheists were arrogant. The literal meaning of the sentence suggests that this witness lived during the time of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and believed in him while others took the path of arrogance.

Others have said this man was one of the scholars of the People of the Book who lived in Mecca. Although followers of the Jewish and Christian faiths were fewer in Mecca, it is not as if none were there. Nevertheless, it is not clear who this scholar of the Children of Israel was or what his name was. This interpretation also seems less suitable given that no famous scholar from the People of the Book existed in Mecca at the time of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله appearance, and history has not mentioned such a name [4]. Of course, this interpretation and the previous one have the advantage of being consistent with the entire Surah Al-Ahqaf being Meccan.

A third interpretation, accepted by most commentators, is that this witness was the famous Jewish scholar "Abdullah bin Salam," who believed in Medina and joined the Muslims. It is mentioned in a Hadith that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, along with some of his companions in Medina, entered a synagogue (their place of worship) during one of the Jewish holidays. They were displeased by the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله arrival. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "O assembly of Jews, show me twelve men from among you who will testify to the oneness of Allah and the prophethood of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله, so that Allah may remove His wrath from all the Jews of the world." They remained silent and no one answered. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله repeated this three times, and each time they all remained silent. Then he said: "You have refused to state the truth, but by Allah, I am 'al-Hashir' and 'al-Aqib' (titles for the Prophet mentioned in the Torah), whether you believe or deny me." Then the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله turned to leave, but before he had stepped out, a man came behind him and said, "O Muhammad! Wait." The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله stopped. Then the man turned to the assembly of Jews and said: "What kind of man do you consider me to be?" They said: "By Allah, we have no man among us more knowledgeable than you, your father, or your grandfather regarding our scripture." Then he added: "I take Allah as witness that he is the same Prophet mentioned in the Torah and the Gospel!" When the Jews saw this, they said: "You are lying," and they hurled foul and bad words at him. The Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله said: "You are lying; your denial after your admission will never be accepted" (and this man was none other than Abdullah bin Salam). And here the above verse (قُلْ أَ رَأَيْتُمْ إِنْ كانَ مِنْ عِنْدِ اللَّهِ‌ ...) was revealed [5]. According to this interpretation, this verse was revealed in Medina even though the Surah is Meccan. This is not unique to the verse under discussion; in other Surahs of the Quran, Meccan verses are occasionally seen within Medinan Surahs or vice versa, which shows that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله sometimes ordered a verse that was compatible with the content of a Surah to be placed among its verses regardless of its date of revelation. This interpretation seems more appropriate in several respects.

[1] The phrase "in iftaraytuhu" is a conditional sentence whose result (jaza) is omitted and is implied as: "If I have fabricated it, He would seize me and hasten my punishment." [2] Tafsir al-Fakhr al-Razi, Vol. 28, p. 8. [3] The result of the conditional sentence (in kana min 'indillah) is omitted and is implied as "who is more astray than you." [4] The use of "witness" (shahid) in the indefinite form here is for magnification (ta'zim) and indicates he was a well-known and great person. [5] Tafsir al-Maraghi, Vol. 26, p. 14.

Ayahs

وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لِلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَوْ كَانَ خَيْرًا مَّا سَبَقُونَآ إِلَيْهِ ۚ وَإِذْ لَمْ يَهْتَدُوا۟ بِهِۦ فَسَيَقُولُونَ هَـٰذَآ إِفْكٌ قَدِيمٌ (١١)
The faithless say about the faithful, ‘Had it been [anything] good, they would not have taken the lead over us toward [accepting] it.’ And since they could not find the way to it, they will say, ‘It is an ancient lie.’ (11)
وَمِن قَبْلِهِۦ كِتَـٰبُ مُوسَىٰٓ إِمَامًا وَرَحْمَةً ۚ وَهَـٰذَا كِتَـٰبٌ مُّصَدِّقٌ لِّسَانًا عَرَبِيًّا لِّيُنذِرَ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ وَبُشْرَىٰ لِلْمُحْسِنِينَ (١٢)
Yet before it the Book of Moses was a guide and mercy, and this is a Book in the Arabic language, which confirms it, [sent] to warn the wrongdoers, and is a [bearer of] good news for the virtuous. (12)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوا۟ رَبُّنَا ٱللَّهُ ثُمَّ ٱسْتَقَـٰمُوا۟ فَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ (١٣)
Those who say, ‘Our Lord is Allah,’ and then remain steadfast, they will have no fear, nor will they grieve. (13)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَنَّةِ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا جَزَآءًۢ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (١٤)
They shall be the inhabitants of paradise, remaining in it [forever]—a reward for what they used to do. (14)

Occasion of Revelation

Commentators have narrated several occasions of revelation for the first verse under discussion:

  1. This verse concerns "Abu Dharr al-Ghifari," who embraced Islam in Mecca, and his tribe (Banu Ghifar) followed him in faith. Since the Banu Ghifar were desert-dwellers and poor, the elite of the Quraysh—who were wealthy city-dwellers—said: "If Islam were something good, these lowly people would not have preceded us in it!" It was then that the above verse was revealed to answer them.

  2. There was a Roman slave girl in Mecca named Dhi al-Nayrah [^1] who responded to the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) call to Islam. The leaders of the Quraysh said: "If what Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله) has brought were something good, the likes of 'Dhi al-Nayrah' would not have preceded us!"

  3. A group of desert-dwelling tribes around Mecca turned to Islam before the city folk. The nobles of Mecca said: "If Islam were something good, these camel-herders would not have preceded us!"

[^1]: "Dhi al-Nayrah" was among the women who accepted Islam early on, and for this reason, Abu Jahl used to torture and harass her.

  1. A group of pure-hearted but poor and destitute men, such as "Bilal," "Suhaib," and "Ammar," embraced Islam with open arms. The nobles of Mecca said: "Is it possible for Muhammad's (صلى الله عليه وآله) religion to be something good and yet these people surpass us in it?!"

  2. When "Abdullah bin Salam" and some of his companions believed, a group of arrogant Jews said: "If Islam were good, they would not have been the first to join it." [^1]

The first four occasions of revelation can be summarized in one sentence: Islam was rapidly welcomed by the poor, the desert-dwellers, and the destitute, because they had no illegitimate interests at stake, their minds were not filled with the wind of arrogance, and they possessed hearts purer than the hedonistic and self-indulgent affluent class.

This warm reception by this group—which was one of the greatest strengths of this divine religion—was viewed by the arrogant oppressors as a major weakness. They said: "What kind of religion is this whose followers are a mass of poor desert-dwellers, slaves, and bondwomen?! If it were a rational school of thought, the lower and degraded levels of society should never have welcomed it while we, who are at the top and are the 'eyes and lights' of society, remain behind!"

Interestingly, this deviant way of thinking remains one of the most common mindsets today among the arrogant wealthy and the affluent hedonists regarding religion, as they say: "Religion is for the poor and the barefooted; they are good for each other, while we are on a much higher level!" The Quran, in the verses under discussion, provides an adequate response to them, which you will hear in the commentary.

As for the fifth occasion of revelation mentioned above—referring to Abdullah bin Salam and his companions—although it has been narrated from most commentators according to Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan and Qurtubi in his Tafsir, it seems unlikely for two reasons:

First, the expression "those who disbelieve" (الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا) in an absolute sense is usually applied to the polytheists, not the People of the Book (Jews and Christians).

Second, "Abdullah bin Salam" was not a person of low status among the Jews such that they would say: "If Islam were a good religion, he and his companions would not have preceded us."

Commentary

The Condition for Victory is Faith and Steadfastness

These verses continue to discuss, analyze, and rebuke the actions, words, and deviations of the disbelievers. First, it points to their arrogant and illogical statement: "And those who disbelieve say of those who believe, 'If it had been [any] good, they would not have preceded us to it.'" (وَ قالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَوْ كانَ خَيْراً ما سَبَقُونا إِلَيْهِ) [^1] They claimed these are just a handful of poor, lowly, rural people and slaves with little knowledge! How could they understand the truth and turn to it, while we—the luminaries of this society—remain heedless and unaware of it?

[^1]: Regarding the meaning of the "Lam" in li-lladhina amanu, commentators have discussed much, but the most appropriate possibility is that "Lam" means "concerning" (fi). Thus, the meaning becomes: "The disbelievers said concerning the believers..." In this case, no problem arises regarding the third-person verb sabaquna. Some have considered it a "Lam of causality," and others have said the believers are the ones being addressed and sabaquna means sabaqtumuna (you would not have preceded us).

They were oblivious to the fact that the fault lay within themselves, not in the religion of Islam. If the veils of pride and arrogance had not fallen over their hearts, if they were not intoxicated by wealth, status, and lust, and if self-superiority and self-centeredness had allowed them to investigate the truth—and if they had been like the pure-hearted poor, seeking and demanding the truth—they too would have been quickly attracted to Islam.

Therefore, at the end of the verse, He answers them with this subtle expression: "And since they are not guided by it, they will say, 'This is an ancient falsehood.'" (وَ إِذْ لَمْ يَهْتَدُوا بِهِ فَسَيَقُولُونَ هذا إِفْكٌ قَدِيمٌ) [^1] That is, they did not want to be guided by the Quran, not that the Quran's guidance was lacking.

The expression "ancient falsehood" (ifkun qadim) is similar to another slander narrated from them in the Quran where they said: "These are the legends of the ancients" (asatir al-awwalin) (Furqan: 5). Furthermore, the use of sayakulun (they will say) in the future/present tense indicates that they continuously leveled this accusation against the Quran, using it as a cover for their lack of faith.

Then, He proceeds to another proof for the truthfulness of the Quran and the negation of the polytheists' slander that it is an "ancient falsehood," saying: "And before it was the Scripture of Moses as a guide and a mercy. And this is a confirming Book..." (وَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِ كِتابُ مُوسى‌ إِماماً وَ رَحْمَةً وَ هذا كِتابٌ مُصَدِّقٌ).

With this being the case, how can you say this is an ancient falsehood?! Repeatedly in the verses of the Quran, emphasis is placed on the fact that the Quran confirms the Torah and the Gospel; that is, it is consistent with the signs mentioned in these two Heavenly Books regarding the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) and his Scripture. These signs were so precise that the Quran says: "Those to whom We gave the Scripture recognize him as they recognize their own sons" (Baqarah: 146).

A meaning similar to the verse under discussion also appears in Surah Hud, verse 17: "Is he [to be compared to one] who rests upon a clear proof from his Lord, and a witness from Him recites it, and before it was the Scripture of Moses as a guide and a mercy? Those [latter] believe in it..." (Hud: 17).

The description "as a guide and a mercy" (imaman wa rahmatan) might be because the mention of a "leader" (imam) sometimes brings to mind the idea of a difficult or harsh duty due to the memories people had of their worldly leaders. However, the mention of "mercy" corrects this association, stating that the leadership of this Imam is coupled with mercy; even if it brings obligations, they too are a mercy—and what mercy is greater than the cultivation of their souls?

Following that, He adds: "...in an Arabic tongue" (لِساناً عَرَبِيًّا), meaning in an eloquent and clear Arabic language so that everyone may benefit from it.

At the end of the verse, He explains the ultimate goal of the revelation of the Quran in two short phrases: "...to warn those who have wronged and as good tidings for the doers of good." (لِيُنْذِرَ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا وَ بُشْرى‌ لِلْمُحْسِنِينَ).

Given that yundhira (to warn) is a present/future tense verb indicating continuity, it becomes clear that the Quran's warning, like its glad tidings, is permanent and continuous. It cautions and warns the wrongdoers throughout history and constantly gives glad tidings to the doers of good.

It is noteworthy that "doers of good" (muhsinin) is placed as the opposite of "those who have wronged" (dhalimu), because "wrong" (dhulm) here has a broad meaning encompassing every kind of evil and transgression, which is naturally either a wrong against others or a wrong against one's own self.

The next verse is, in fact, an explanation of the "doers of good" (muhsinin) mentioned in the previous verse. It says: "Indeed, those who have said, 'Our Lord is Allah,' and then remained on a right course—there will be no fear concerning them, nor will they grieve." (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ قالُوا رَبُّنَا اللَّهُ ثُمَّ اسْتَقامُوا فَلا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَ لا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ) [^1] In reality, all levels of faith and all righteous deeds are gathered in these two phrases. For "Monotheism" (Tawhid) is the foundation of all correct beliefs, and all principles of faith return to the root of Tawhid. "Steadfastness" (istiqamah), patience, and endurance are the roots of all righteous deeds, as we know that all good deeds can be summarized under three headings: "patience in obedience," "patience against sin," and "patience during calamity."

[^1]: Alladhina qalu rabbuna Allah... is the subject (mubtada), and the phrase la khawfa 'alayhim is its predicate (khabar). Normally, the particle fa does not precede the predicate unless, as in this verse, the concept of a condition is implied in the sentence.

Therefore, the "doers of good" are those who, in terms of belief, are on the path of Monotheism, and in terms of action, are on the path of steadfastness and patience.

Naturally, such individuals have no fear of future events, nor any grief over the past.

A similar point is mentioned in verse 30 of Surah Fussilat (with more detail), where it says: "Indeed, those who have said, 'Our Lord is Allah' and then remained on a right course—the angels will descend upon them, [saying], 'Do not fear and do not grieve but receive the good tidings of Paradise, which you were promised.'"

This verse adds two things: first, that the glad tidings of the absence of fear and grief are given to them by the angels (whereas the verse under discussion remains silent on this), and second, in addition to the negation of fear and grief, the glad tidings of the promised Paradise are mentioned in Surah Fussilat, whereas in the current context, it is mentioned in the following verse.

In any case, these two verses pursue the same point—one more concisely and the other more elaborately.

In the Tafsir of Ali bin Ibrahim, we read regarding the verse "Indeed, those who have said, 'Our Lord is Allah,' and then remained on a right course" that he said: "They remained steadfast upon the Wilayah of Ali, the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام)."

This is because continuing the path of the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام) in terms of knowledge, action, justice, and piety—especially in dark and gloomy eras—is a difficult task that is impossible without steadfastness. Therefore, it is considered one of the clear applications of the verse, not that its meaning is restricted to this alone, excluding steadfastness in Jihad, obedience to the Lord, and struggling against the self and Satan.

Under verse 30 of Surah Fussilat, we have provided an extensive explanation regarding the issue of "steadfastness." [^1]

In the final verse under discussion, He gives the most important glad tidings to the righteous monotheists, saying: "Those are the companions of Paradise, abiding eternally therein" (أُولئِكَ أَصْحابُ الْجَنَّةِ خالِدِينَ فِيها). "As reward for what they used to do." (جَزاءً بِما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ).

The appearance of the verse, as some have inferred, conveys the concept of exclusivity; that is, the inhabitants of Paradise are only those who walk the path of Monotheism and steadfastness. Naturally, other individuals who have been tainted by sins—though they may eventually enter Paradise due to their faith—are not initially "companions of Paradise."

The term "companions" (as-hab) refers to their permanent companionship with the blessings of Paradise.

The expression "As reward for what they used to do" serves as proof on one hand that "Paradise is given for a price, not for an excuse," and on the other hand, it points to the principle of human free will and choice.

[^1]: Refer to Volume 20 of Tafsir Nemouneh, Surah Fussilat, verse 30.

Ayahs

وَوَصَّيْنَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ بِوَٰلِدَيْهِ إِحْسَـٰنًا ۖ حَمَلَتْهُ أُمُّهُۥ كُرْهًا وَوَضَعَتْهُ كُرْهًا ۖ وَحَمْلُهُۥ وَفِصَـٰلُهُۥ ثَلَـٰثُونَ شَهْرًا ۚ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا بَلَغَ أَشُدَّهُۥ وَبَلَغَ أَرْبَعِينَ سَنَةً قَالَ رَبِّ أَوْزِعْنِىٓ أَنْ أَشْكُرَ نِعْمَتَكَ ٱلَّتِىٓ أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَىَّ وَعَلَىٰ وَٰلِدَىَّ وَأَنْ أَعْمَلَ صَـٰلِحًا تَرْضَىٰهُ وَأَصْلِحْ لِى فِى ذُرِّيَّتِىٓ ۖ إِنِّى تُبْتُ إِلَيْكَ وَإِنِّى مِنَ ٱلْمُسْلِمِينَ (١٥)
We have enjoined man to be kind to his parents. His mother has carried him in travail, and bore him in travail, and his gestation and weaning take thirty months. When he comes of age and reaches forty years, he says, ‘My Lord! Inspire me to give thanks for Your blessing with which You have blessed me and my parents, and that I may do righteous deeds which please You, and invest my descendants with righteousness. Indeed I have turned to you in penitence, and I am one of the Muslims.’ (15)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ نَتَقَبَّلُ عَنْهُمْ أَحْسَنَ مَا عَمِلُوا۟ وَنَتَجَاوَزُ عَن سَيِّـَٔاتِهِمْ فِىٓ أَصْحَـٰبِ ٱلْجَنَّةِ ۖ وَعْدَ ٱلصِّدْقِ ٱلَّذِى كَانُوا۟ يُوعَدُونَ (١٦)
Such are the ones from whom We accept the best of what they do, and overlook their misdeeds, [who will be] among the inhabitants of paradise—a true promise which they had been given. (16)

Commentary

O Man! Be Kind to Your Mother and Father

These verses and the following ones are, in fact, an explanation regarding the two groups—the "wrongdoers" (Zalim) and the "doers of good" (Muhsin)—whose fates were briefly mentioned in the preceding verses.

First, it addresses the state of the "doers of good," beginning with the issue of kindness toward parents and gratitude for their hardships, which serves as a prelude to thanking the Lord. It states: "And We have enjoined upon man kindness to his parents" (وَ وَصَّيْنَا الْإِنْسانَ بِوالِدَيْهِ إِحْساناً) [1]. The word wasiyyah and tawsiyah mean a general recommendation or injunction, and its concept is not limited to instructions related to after death; hence, a group of commentators has interpreted it here to mean "command, instruction, and decree."

Then, it proceeds to the reason for the necessity of gratitude toward the mother, saying: "His mother carried him with hardship and gave birth to him with hardship, and the period of his gestation and weaning is thirty months" (حَمَلَتْهُ أُمُّهُ كُرْهاً وَ وَضَعَتْهُ كُرْهاً وَ حَمْلُهُ وَ فِصالُهُ ثَلاثُونَ شَهْراً).

[1]: Tawsiyah usually takes two objects, but the second object is accompanied by the preposition ba or ila. Therefore, ihsanan cannot be the second object in the above verse unless we take wassayna to mean alzamna (We made obligatory), which takes two objects without prepositions, or we assume a deleted part of the verse and say the estimation is: "We enjoined man that he should act with kindness toward his parents." In this case, ihsanan is a "cognate accusative" (maf'ul mutlaq) for the deleted verb.

During these thirty months, the "mother" performs the greatest sacrifice and devotion for her child.

From the very first days of conception, the mother's state changes, and hardships follow one after another. A condition known as "morning sickness" occurs, which is one of the most difficult states for a mother. Doctors say this is due to deficiencies that occur in the mother's body because of her sacrifice for the child.

As the fetus grows and develops, it takes more nutrients from the mother's life-essence, even affecting her bones and nerves. Sometimes it deprives her of sleep, food, rest, and peace. In the final stages of pregnancy, walking and even sitting or standing become difficult for her. Yet, with complete patience and out of love for the child who will soon open its eyes to the world and smile at her, she endures all these hardships.

The time of childbirth arrives, which is one of the most difficult moments of a mother's life, to the point that sometimes the mother gives her life for the child.

In any case, once she has laid down her heavy burden, another difficult period begins: the period of constant, day-and-night care for the child. This is a time when she must respond to all the needs of an infant who has no power to express its needs. If it is in pain, it cannot specify the location of the pain; if it suffers from hunger, thirst, heat, or cold, it cannot articulate it, except by crying and shedding tears. The mother, with complete inquisitiveness and patience, must identify and fulfill each of these needs one by one.

Cleaning the child during this period is an exhausting task, and providing its food—which is drawn from the mother's own life-essence—is a great sacrifice.

The various illnesses that afflict the newborn during this period, which the mother must confront with extraordinary patience, are another challenge.

The fact that the Quran mentions only the mother's hardships here and does not mention the father is not due to a lack of importance on his part, for the father also shares in many of these difficulties. However, because the mother has a greater share, she is emphasized more.

Here, a question arises: in verse 233 of Surah Al-Baqarah, the period of breastfeeding is mentioned as two full years (24 months): "Mothers shall suckle their children for two whole years, for those who wish to complete the term of suckling" (وَ الْوالِداتُ يُرْضِعْنَ أَوْلادَهُنَّ حَوْلَيْنِ كامِلَيْنِ لِمَنْ أَرادَ أَنْ يُتِمَّ الرَّضاعَةَ).

Yet, the total "period of gestation and weaning" in the verse under discussion is mentioned as only thirty months. Is it possible for the gestation period to be six months?

Inspired by Islamic narrations, jurists and commentators have answered: Yes, the minimum period of gestation is 6 months, and the maximum period of beneficial suckling is 24 months. It has even been narrated from a group of ancient physicians such as Galen and Avicenna that they personally witnessed cases where a child was born after six months.

Furthermore, it can be inferred from this Quranic expression that as the gestation period decreases, the breastfeeding period should increase, such that the total encompasses a full 30 months. It is also narrated from Ibn Abbas that if a woman's pregnancy lasts 9 months, she should breastfeed the child for 21 months, and if the pregnancy is 6 months, she should breastfeed for 24 months.

Natural law also necessitates this, as the deficiencies of the gestation period must be compensated for during the breastfeeding period.

Then it adds: Human life continues "until, when he reaches his full strength and reaches forty years" (حَتَّى إِذا بَلَغَ أَشُدَّهُ وَ بَلَغَ أَرْبَعِينَ سَنَةً) [1]. Some commentators consider "reaching full strength" (bulugh al-ashudd) to be synonymous with reaching forty years, used for emphasis. However, it appears that "reaching full strength" refers to "physical maturity," while reaching "forty years" refers to "intellectual and rational maturity." It is well known that a human often reaches the stage of perfected intellect at forty, and it is said that most prophets were commissioned to their mission at the age of forty.

Additionally, there is discussion regarding the age of physical maturity. Some consider it the well-known age of puberty, which is also mentioned in verse 34 of Surah Al-Isra regarding orphans, while some narrations explicitly state it is the age of eighteen.

Of course, there is no obstacle to this expression having different meanings in different contexts, which are clarified by surrounding clues.

In a Hadith, it is stated: "Indeed, Satan rubs his hand over the face of one who exceeds forty and does not repent, saying: 'May my father be sacrificed for a face that will never succeed!'" (meaning the light of salvation is not in this person's forehead!) [1] It is also narrated from Ibn Abbas: "Whoever reaches forty years and his good does not outweigh his evil, let him prepare for the Fire!"

In any case, the Quran adds following this: When this worthy and faithful person reaches forty years, he asks three things of Allah. First, he says: "My Lord, inspire me and grant me the success to be grateful for Your favor which You have bestowed upon me and upon my parents" (قالَ رَبِّ أَوْزِعْنِي أَنْ أَشْكُرَ نِعْمَتَكَ الَّتِي أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيَّ وَ عَلى‌ والِدَيَ‌) [2]. This expression shows that a person of faith at such an age becomes aware of both the depth and breadth of Allah's blessings upon him, and the services his parents rendered to him to reach this point. At this age, he usually becomes a parent himself and sees with his own eyes their exhausting and sacrificial efforts, involuntarily remembering them and offering gratitude to Allah on their behalf.

In the second request, he says: "O Allah! Grant me success to do righteous deeds that please You" (وَ أَنْ أَعْمَلَ صالِحاً تَرْضاهُ‌).

And finally, in his third request, he says: "O Allah! Make righteousness and goodness continue in my offspring" (وَ أَصْلِحْ لِي فِي ذُرِّيَّتِي‌).

[1]: Hatta here is the limit for a deleted sentence, estimated as: "And the human lived and his life continued until he reached his full strength." Some have considered it the limit for wassayna or for the issue of "parental care," but both seem unlikely, as Allah's injunction regarding kindness to parents does not end at forty, nor does parental care for a child continue only until forty.

[1]: Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 26, p. 17. [2]: Awzi'ni is from the root izaa', which has several meanings: to inspire, to restrain from deviation, to create love and interest, and to grant success.

The expression "for me" (li) implicitly indicates that the righteousness and goodness of my children should be such that its results also benefit me.

The expression "in my offspring" (fi dhurriyyati) in an absolute sense refers to the continuation of righteousness and good deeds throughout his entire lineage.

It is interesting that in the first prayer, he includes his parents; in the third prayer, he includes his children; but in the second prayer, he prays for himself. Such is the righteous person: while looking at himself with one eye, he looks at those who have rights over him with the other.

At the end of the verse, he declares two points, each representing an effective practical program. He says: "O Lord! At this age, I turn back to You and repent" (إِنِّي تُبْتُ إِلَيْكَ‌).

I have reached a stage where the lines of my life must be determined and continue until the end of my life. Yes, I have reached the threshold of forty, and how ugly and unseemly it is for a servant like me not to come toward You and not to wash myself from sins with the water of repentance.

Furthermore, he says: "And indeed, I am of the Muslims" (وَ إِنِّي مِنَ الْمُسْلِمِينَ‌).

In reality, these two sentences are a support for those three prayers, and their meaning is: Since I have repented and am in absolute submission to Your command, You also be generous and include me in those blessings.

The next verse is an eloquent statement of the reward and recompense for this group of grateful, righteous, and repentant believers, mentioning three important rewards.

First, He says: "Those are the ones from whom We will accept the best of what they did" (أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ نَتَقَبَّلُ عَنْهُمْ أَحْسَنَ ما عَمِلُوا).

What glad tidings could be greater than this: that the Great, Powerful, and Gracious Allah accepts the deed of a weak and insignificant servant? This itself, aside from other effects, is a great honor and a sublime spiritual gift.

Since Allah accepts all good deeds, why does He say: "He accepts the best of their deeds"?! In response to this question, a group of commentators has said: "The best of deeds" refers to obligatory and recommended acts, as opposed to permissible acts (mubahat), which are good deeds but not something that is "accepted" in the sense of receiving a specific reward [1]. Another answer is that Allah makes their best deeds the criterion for acceptance, and by His grace and mercy, He counts even their second-rate or less important deeds as first-rate deeds. This is exactly like a buyer who, out of grace and generosity, calculates various goods offered by a seller at the price of the highest quality item. Nothing is surprising when it comes to the grace and kindness of Allah.

The second gift is their purification; He says: "And We will overlook their misdeeds" (وَ نَتَجاوَزُ عَنْ سَيِّئاتِهِمْ‌).

While they are "among the companions of Paradise" (فِي أَصْحابِ الْجَنَّةِ) [2]. This is the third divine gift to them: that despite having had slips, He washes them clean and places them alongside the good and pure ones who are near to His presence.

Furthermore, it is inferred from this expression that "companions of Paradise" here refers to the near servants who never let the dust of sin settle on their robes, and these repentant believers, after divine forgiveness, are placed alongside them and in their shadow.

At the end of the verse, to emphasize these mentioned blessings, He adds: "This is the promise of truth which they were continuously promised" (وَعْدَ الصِّدْقِ الَّذِي كانُوا يُوعَدُونَ‌) [1]. How could it not be a promise of truth when breaking a promise is either due to regret and ignorance or due to weakness and inability, and Allah is sanctified from all such things.

Key Points

1- A Portrait of a Believing Inhabitant of Paradise

These verses are a portrait of a believing inhabitant of Paradise who first traversed his physical growth and then his stage of intellectual perfection. Afterward, he reached the station of gratitude for the Lord's blessings and gratitude for the exhausting efforts of his parents. He repents from slips in a timely manner and strives to perform righteous deeds, including the upbringing of children. Finally, he ascends to the station of absolute submission to the divine command, and this very matter causes him to be immersed in Allah's mercy, forgiveness, and various blessings.

Yes, a person of Paradise must be recognized by these attributes.

2- The Expression "We Enjoined Man" (Wassayna al-Insan)

This points to the fact that the issue of kindness to parents is among the human principles that even those who are not bound by any religion or creed are drawn to by the inspiration of their innate nature (fitrah). Therefore, those who disregard this great duty are not only not true Muslims, but the name "human" is not even worthy of them.

[1]: Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan, Allamah Tabataba'i in Al-Mizan, Fakhr Razi in Tafsir al-Kabir, and others under the verse discussed. [2]: Fi ashab al-jannah relates to a deleted part that is a "state" (hal) for the pronoun "them," estimated as: "while they are present among the companions of Paradise." [1]: Wa'd al-sidq is a cognate accusative for a deleted verb, estimated as: "He promises them the promise of truth which they were promised through the tongues of the prophets and messengers."

3- The Expression "Kindness" (Ihsanan)

(Considering that the indefinite noun in such cases is used to express greatness) This indicates that by Allah's command, great and prominent acts of kindness must be performed in return for the great services of parents.

4- Description of the Mother's Pain and Suffering

The description of the mother's pain and suffering in the path of raising the child is both because it is more perceptible and tangible, and because the mother's efforts are of greater significance compared to the father's. For this reason, in Islamic narrations, more emphasis has been placed on the mother.

In a Hadith, it is mentioned that a man came to the Messenger of Allah and asked: "To whom should I be kind?" He said: "Your mother." He asked: "Then who?" He said: "Your mother." He asked: "Then who?" He said: "Your mother." On the fourth time when he repeated the question, he said: "Your father!" [1]

In another Hadith, it is mentioned that a man was carrying his old and weak mother on his shoulders, performing circumambulation (Tawaf). At that moment, he came to the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) and asked: "Have I fulfilled her right?" The Prophet replied: "No, not even for a single one of her sighs [during labor]" [1].

5- The Quran's Emphasis on Family Bonds

In the verses of the Quran, great importance is given to family bonds, respect and honor for parents, and attention to the upbringing of children, all of which are mentioned in the above verses. This is because the great human society is formed from smaller units called "families," just as a large building is formed from rooms, and then from stones and bricks.

Naturally, the more cohesion and strength these small units possess, the greater the strength of the society's foundation will be. One of the causes of social disorders in the industrial societies of our time is the disintegration of the family system, where there is neither respect from children, nor affection from parents, nor a bond of love and emotion from spouses.

The painful sight of nursing homes in today's industrial societies—which are centers for weak parents who are incapacitated and rejected by their families—is a very eloquent witness to this bitter reality.

Men and women who, after a lifetime of service and delivering multiple children to society, are completely cast out during the days when they have an intense need for their children's affection and help. There, they count the days waiting for death, their eyes fixed on the door for an acquaintance to enter—an expectation that perhaps occurs no more than once or twice a year! Truly, the thought of such a state makes life bitter for a human from its very beginning, and this is the way of the material world and civilization devoid of faith and religion.

[1]: Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 26, p. 16. [1]: Fi Zilal, Vol. 7, p. 415.

6- Commentary on the Phrase "That I May Do Righteous Deeds That Please You"

The phrase "أَنْ أَعْمَلَ صالِحاً تَرْضاهُ" (that I may do righteous deeds that please You) expresses the reality that a righteous deed is something that brings about Allah's pleasure. The expression "the best of what they did" (ahsan ma 'amilu), which appears in several verses of the Holy Quran, expresses Allah's limitless grace, which, in the position of rewarding His servants, takes their best deeds as the criterion and accepts all of them based on that.

Ayahs

وَٱلَّذِى قَالَ لِوَٰلِدَيْهِ أُفٍّ لَّكُمَآ أَتَعِدَانِنِىٓ أَنْ أُخْرَجَ وَقَدْ خَلَتِ ٱلْقُرُونُ مِن قَبْلِى وَهُمَا يَسْتَغِيثَانِ ٱللَّهَ وَيْلَكَ ءَامِنْ إِنَّ وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ حَقٌّ فَيَقُولُ مَا هَـٰذَآ إِلَّآ أَسَـٰطِيرُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (١٧)
As for him who says to his parents, ‘Fie on you! Do you promise me that I shall be raised [from the dead] when generations have passed away before me?’ And they invoke Allah’s help [and say]: ‘Woe to you! Believe! Indeed Allah’s promise is true.’ But he says, ‘These are nothing but myths of the ancients.’ (17)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ حَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْقَوْلُ فِىٓ أُمَمٍ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِهِم مِّنَ ٱلْجِنِّ وَٱلْإِنسِ ۖ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ خَـٰسِرِينَ (١٨)
Such are the ones against whom the word has become due, along with the nations of jinn and humans that have passed away before them. They were the losers. (18)
وَلِكُلٍّ دَرَجَـٰتٌ مِّمَّا عَمِلُوا۟ ۖ وَلِيُوَفِّيَهُمْ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ وَهُمْ لَا يُظْلَمُونَ (١٩)
For everyone there are degrees [of merit] pertaining to what he has done: He will recompense them fully for their works, and they will not be wronged. (19)

Commentary

Those Who Trample the Rights of Parents

In the preceding verses, the discussion pertained to the believers who, in the light of faith, righteous deeds, gratitude for Divine blessings, and mindfulness of the rights of parents and children, find their way to the station of Divine proximity and become recipients of His special favors.

However, the verses under discussion speak of those who stand at the opposite pole—individuals devoid of faith, ungrateful, and rebellious toward their parents. It states:

"And the one who says to his parents, 'Uff to you! Do you promise me that I shall be brought forth [from the grave], while generations have already passed before me [and none have returned]?'" (وَ الَّذِي قالَ لِوالِدَيْهِ أُفٍّ لَكُما أَ تَعِدانِنِي أَنْ أُخْرَجَ وَ قَدْ خَلَتِ الْقُرُونُ مِنْ قَبْلِي) [^1] Yet the believing parents do not surrender to this headstrong child; "they cry out for Allah's help [saying]: 'Woe to you! Believe!

[^1]: "And the one who said..." (و الذى قال) is the subject (mubtada), and its predicate (khabar), according to many commentators, is "Those are the ones..." (اولئك الذين) which appears in the following verse. The singular form of the subject does not conflict with the plural form of "those" (اولئك), as it refers to a genus or category. However, there is also the possibility that the predicate is omitted, implied as: "And in contrast to those whose description has passed is the one who said to his parents..." In this case, the next verse is independent, just as the verse "Those are the ones from whom We accept..." is independent.

Indeed, the promise of Allah is true'": (وَ هُما يَسْتَغِيثانِ اللَّهَ وَيْلَكَ آمِنْ إِنَّ وَعْدَ اللَّهِ حَقٌّ).

But he continues in his stubbornness and obstinacy, and with arrogance and indifference, "he says: 'This is nothing but the fables of the ancients!'" (فَيَقُولُ ما هذا إِلَّا أَساطِيرُ الْأَوَّلِينَ).

He claims that the talk of Resurrection and a Day of Reckoning is merely the superstitions and false tales of past peoples, and that he will never submit to it.

Several characteristics of this group can be derived from this verse:

First, disrespect and insolence toward the status of parents, for "Uff" (اف) originally means anything filthy and contaminated, and is uttered in a context of insult and contempt. [^1] Some have also said it refers to the dirt that collects under the fingernails, which is both filthy and insignificant. [^2] Second, they not only lack faith in the Resurrection and the Day of Rising but also mock it, considering it part of myths and superstitious imaginings.

Another of their traits is that they lack a listening ear; they do not submit to the truth, and their souls are filled with pride, ignorance, and selfishness.

Indeed, his compassionate parents exert every effort to save him from the whirlpool of ignorance and heedlessness so that this beloved child does not fall into the painful Divine punishment, yet he persists and insists on his disbelief, until finally, they are forced to leave him be.

[^1]: Mufradat of Raghib. [^2]: Regarding the meaning of "Uff," we have provided further discussions in Volume 12, page 83 (Surah Al-Isra, Verse 23).

Just as the reward of the righteous believers was explained in the previous verses, here the end of the bold and headstrong disbelievers is described:

"Those are the ones against whom the word [of punishment] has become justified, among the nations of jinn and mankind that passed before them; indeed, they were losers" (أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ حَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ الْقَوْلُ فِي أُمَمٍ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ مِنَ الْجِنِّ وَ الْإِنْسِ) [^1] "For they were all losers" (إِنَّهُمْ كانُوا خاسِرِينَ).

What loss could be worse than losing all the capital of one's existence and purchasing the wrath and anger of Allah for oneself?

In comparing these two groups—the people of Hell and the people of Paradise—we encounter these points in these verses:

The former traverse the stages of growth and perfection, while the latter lose all their capital and are losers.

The former are appreciative and grateful, even toward their parents, while the latter are ungrateful, bold, and disrespectful even toward their parents.

The former are "with those near to Allah" in Paradise, and the latter are "among the ranks of faithless nations" in Hell, each being joined to their own kind.

The former repent for their slips and submit to the truth, while the latter are rebellious, defiant, selfish, and arrogant.

It is noteworthy that this stubborn group relies on the condition of past nations in their deviations, and in Hell, they will be gathered with them.

[^1]: The phrase "the word has become justified against them" (حَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ الْقَوْلُ) refers to the statement Allah made regarding the punishment of disbelievers and criminals, implied as: "The word became justified against them that they are the people of the Fire..." and "among the nations" (فى امم) is in the position of a circumstantial clause (hal).

In the final verse under discussion, the text first points to the difference in degrees and ranks of each of these two groups, saying: "For each there are degrees according to what they have done" (وَ لِكُلٍّ دَرَجاتٌ مِمَّا عَمِلُوا) [^1]. It is not the case that the people of Paradise or the people of Hell are all at one level; rather, they have different ranks according to their deeds, and in proportion to the sincerity of their intentions and the extent of their knowledge. The principle of justice governs here precisely.

"Darajat" (درجات) is the plural of "darajah" (درجة), usually referring to steps one climbs upward, while "darakat" (دركات) is the plural of "dark" (درك), referring to steps one descends downward. Therefore, for Paradise, "darajat" is used, and for Hell, "darakat" is used. However, in the verse under discussion where both are mentioned together, out of respect for the status of the people of Paradise, both are referred to as "darajat," which is a linguistic case of "taghlib" (dominance of one term over another). [^2] Then it adds: "The goal is that Allah may repay them for their deeds in full" (وَ لِيُوَفِّيَهُمْ أَعْمالَهُمْ).

This expression is another allusion to the "embodiment of deeds," where a person's actions will be with him; his good deeds will be a source of mercy and peace, and his evil deeds a source of calamity, distress, suffering, and punishment.

And finally, as an emphasis, it says: "And they will not be wronged" (وَ هُمْ لا يُظْلَمُونَ).

For they receive their own deeds; in such a case, how can injustice or oppression be imagined?

Furthermore, their "darajat" and "darakat" are precisely determined, and even the smallest good or bad deed affects their destiny. Under these conditions, injustice has no meaning.

Key Point: How was this verse distorted by the Umayyads?

It is mentioned in a Hadith that Mu'awiyah wrote a letter to Marwan (the governor of Medina) to take the oath of allegiance from the people for his son, Yazid. Abd al-Rahman, the son of Abu Bakr, was present in the assembly and said: "Mu'awiyah wants to do this just like Heraclius and Khosrow (the kings of Rome and Persia), where fathers chose their sons (no matter how unworthy or corrupt) as their successors."

Marwan shouted from the pulpit: "Be silent! You are the one about whom this verse was revealed: 'And the one who said to his parents: Uff to you...'"

Aisha was present; she turned to him and said: "You lie! I know regarding whom this verse was revealed. If you wish, I can tell you his name and lineage. But the Messenger of Allah cursed your father while you were still in your father's loins; therefore, you are the result of the curse of the Messenger of Allah." [^1]

[^1]: "Min" (من) in "from what they did" (مما عملوا) is "nashwiyyah" (originative) or means causality (because of what they did). [^2]: "Dark" (with a sukun on the middle letter) and "darak" (with a fatha) both mean the lowest depth. Sometimes "darak" (with a fatha) is used to mean loss, and "dark" (with a sukun) to mean understanding and perceiving something (fittingly, reaching its depth and reality). [^1]: Abu al-Futuh Razi, Vol. 10, p. 159. Qurtubi has narrated the same tradition with a slight difference (Vol. 9, p. 6017).

Indeed, the "sin" of Abd al-Rahman was that, on one hand, he loved the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (peace be upon him)—something very unpleasant for the Umayyads.

On the other hand, he was strongly opposed to the caliphate becoming hereditary and turning into a monarchy, and he considered taking the oath of allegiance for Yazid a kind of Khosrow-like or Heraclius-like movement.

Therefore, he was attacked by the sworn enemies of Islam, the House of Umayyah, and they distorted the verses of the Holy Quran regarding him.

And what an appropriate response Aisha gave to Marwan: "Allah cursed your father while you were in his loins" (referring to verse 60 of Surah Al-Isra: "...and the cursed tree in the Quran") [^1].

[^1]: For the commentary of this verse, refer to Volume 12 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 171. It should also be noted that Marwan ibn al-Hakam is the son of Abu al-As, who is the son of Umayyah.

Ayah

وَيَوْمَ يُعْرَضُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ عَلَى ٱلنَّارِ أَذْهَبْتُمْ طَيِّبَـٰتِكُمْ فِى حَيَاتِكُمُ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱسْتَمْتَعْتُم بِهَا فَٱلْيَوْمَ تُجْزَوْنَ عَذَابَ ٱلْهُونِ بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَسْتَكْبِرُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ بِغَيْرِ ٱلْحَقِّ وَبِمَا كُنتُمْ تَفْسُقُونَ (٢٠)
The day when the faithless are exposed to the Fire, [they will be told,] ‘You have exhausted your good things in the life of the world and enjoyed them. So today you will be requited with a humiliating punishment for your acting arrogantly in the earth unduly, and because you used to transgress.’ (20)

Commentary

Asceticism and Storing for the Hereafter

This verse continues the discussion of the previous verses regarding the punishment of the disbelievers and criminals, recounting aspects of their physical and spiritual torments. It says:

"And on the Day those who disbelieved are exposed to the Fire, [it will be said], 'You exhausted your pleasures (طيبات) during your worldly life and enjoyed them, so today you will be awarded the punishment of [extreme] humiliation because you were arrogant upon the earth without right and because you were defiantly disobedient.'" (وَ يَوْمَ يُعْرَضُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا عَلَى النَّارِ أَذْهَبْتُمْ طَيِّباتِكُمْ فِي حَياتِكُمُ الدُّنْيا وَ اسْتَمْتَعْتُمْ بِها فَالْيَوْمَ تُجْزَوْنَ عَذابَ الْهُونِ بِما كُنْتُمْ تَسْتَكْبِرُونَ فِي الْأَرْضِ بِغَيْرِ الْحَقِّ وَ بِما كُنْتُمْ تَفْسُقُونَ) [1]

Yes, you were drowned in pleasures and understood nothing except enjoying the material blessings of this world. Because of unconditional freedom in this regard, you denied the "Resurrection" so that your hands would be completely open, and you permitted every kind of oppression and injustice against others to obtain these blessings. Today, you see the retribution for all those whims, ego-worship, arrogance, and sin.

Key Points

1- On the Day of Resurrection, Disbelievers are Exposed to the Fire

This verse states that on the Day of Resurrection, the disbelievers are exposed to the Fire. A similar expression appears in Surah Ghafir (Al-Mu'min), verse 46, regarding the Purgatorial (Barzakh) punishment of the people of Pharaoh: النَّارُ يُعْرَضُونَ عَلَيْها غُدُوًّا وَ عَشِيًّا ("The Fire, they are exposed to it morning and evening").

Meanwhile, in other verses of the Quran, we read that "Hell" is exposed to the disbelievers: و عرضنا جهنم للكافرين عرضا ("And We will present Hell that Day to the disbelievers, a [thorough] presentation") (Al-Kahf: 100).

Therefore, some great commentators have said: On the Day of Resurrection, there are two types of "exposure." Before the reckoning, the Fire is presented to the criminals so that horror and dread fill their entire being (which is itself a spiritual punishment). After the reckoning and sending them toward Hell, they are exposed to the Divine punishment. [1]

Some have also suggested that there is a rhetorical inversion (qalb) in the phrasing, and that "exposing the disbelievers to the Fire" means "exposing the Fire to the disbelievers," because the Fire lacks intellect and consciousness to have disbelievers presented to it, whereas "exposure" usually occurs when the recipient possesses perception and consciousness.

However, this objection can be answered because several verses of the Quran describe a kind of perception and consciousness for Hell, to the extent that Allah speaks to it and it responds. He asks: هل امتلات؟ ("Are you full?"), and it replies: هَلْ مِنْ مَزِيدٍ؟ ("Is there any more?") (Qaf: 30).

The truth is that the reality of "exposure" means the removal of barriers between two things until one is placed at the disposal of the other. Regarding the disbelievers and Hell, the barriers between them are removed; in this case, it can be said both that they are exposed to the Fire and the Fire to them—both expressions are correct.

In any case, there is no need to interpret "exposure" as "entering" the Fire, as Tabarsi mentioned in Majma' al-Bayan. Rather, this exposure is itself a painful and terrifying punishment where the inhabitants of Hell, before entering the Fire, see all parts of Hell from the outside with their own eyes, witness their grim fate, and suffer.

2- The Phrase "You Exhausted Your Pleasures"

The phrase أَذْهَبْتُمْ طَيِّباتِكُمْ means utilizing the pleasures of the world. The expression اذهبتم ("you took away/exhausted") is used because these pleasures and blessings are destroyed and vanish through utilization.

Certainly, enjoying Divine blessings in this world is not a blameworthy act. What is blameworthy is drowning in material pleasures and forgetting the remembrance of Allah and the Resurrection, or the sinful and unconditional utilization of these pleasures and usurping the rights of others in this regard.

It is noteworthy that this specific expression is found only in this verse of the Holy Quran. it hints that sometimes a person foregoes worldly pleasures or uses them only to the extent necessary to gain strength for Divine tasks; in this case, it is as if he has stored these "pleasures" (tayyibat) for his Hereafter.

But often, like cattle, one utilizes them unconditionally and brings them all to destruction, storing nothing for the Hereafter and instead accumulating a burden of sin. The Quran says regarding this group: أَذْهَبْتُمْ طَيِّباتِكُمْ فِي حَياتِكُمُ الدُّنْيا.

In some lexicons, it is narrated that the meaning of the phrase is: "You spent the pure provisions you were given on your desires and worldly pleasures, and did not spend them in the way of Allah's pleasure." [1]

3- "Tayyibat" Includes All Worldly Blessings

"Tayyibat" (pure/good things) has a broad and expansive meaning and includes all worldly blessings, although some commentators have interpreted it only as the strength of youth. However, the truth is that youth can only be one instance of it.

4- The Expression "Punishment of Humiliation"

The expression عذاب الهون (the humiliating and demeaning punishment) is a reaction to their arrogance on earth. Divine punishment is perfectly proportional to the type of sin; those who acted arrogantly toward Allah's creation and even toward the Prophets, and did not submit to any law, must be punished with total humiliation and abasement.

5- Two Sins for the Inhabitants of Hell

At the end of the verse, two sins are mentioned for these inhabitants of Hell: first, "arrogance on the earth," and second, "defiant disobedience" (fisq). It is possible that the first refers to the lack of faith in Divine verses, the mission of the Prophets, and the Resurrection, while the second refers to various types of sins.
