One speaks of abandoning the principles of religion (Usul al-Din), and the other of trampling the branches of religion (Furu' al-Din). [1]

6- The Expression "Without Right"

The expression بغير الحق ("without right") does not mean that arrogance is of two types, "rightful" and "wrongful." Rather, such expressions are usually used for emphasis and have many parallels.

7- The Asceticism of the Great Leaders

In various sources of Hadith and Tafsir, many narrations regarding the asceticism of the great leaders of Islam have been recorded, specifically citing the verse under discussion. For example:

In a Hadith, it is narrated that one day Umar came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله at "Mashrabat Umm Ibrahim" (a place near Medina) while he was sleeping on a mat made of date palm leaves, with part of his blessed body on the ground and a pillow filled with palm fibers under his head. He greeted him and sat down, saying: "You are the Prophet of Allah and the best of His creation, yet Khosrow and Caesar sleep on golden thrones and silk carpets, while you are like this!" The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said:

"They are a people whose pleasures (tayyibat) have been hastened for them, and they are soon to be cut off; but our pleasures have been deferred for us [to the Hereafter]." [1]

In another Hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read: One day, some special halva was brought to him. The Imam refrained from eating it. They asked, "Do you consider it forbidden?" He replied: "No, but I fear that my soul might long for it and I would then seek it out." Then he recited this verse: أَذْهَبْتُمْ طَيِّباتِكُمْ فِي حَياتِكُمُ الدُّنْيا وَ اسْتَمْتَعْتُمْ بِها. [2]

In another Hadith, it is stated: Amir al-Mu'minin عليه السلام desired a dish of roasted liver with soft bread. A year passed and he did not act upon this desire. One day, he asked Imam al-Hasan عليه السلام to prepare it while he was fasting. The food was prepared for Iftar. When he intended to break his fast, a beggar came to the door. The Imam عليه السلام said:

"O my son! Take it to him, lest our record be read tomorrow [on the Day of Judgment] saying: أَذْهَبْتُمْ طَيِّباتِكُمْ فِي حَياتِكُمُ الدُّنْيا وَ اسْتَمْتَعْتُمْ بِها ('You exhausted your pleasures during your worldly life and enjoyed them')." [3]

Ayahs

۞ وَٱذْكُرْ أَخَا عَادٍ إِذْ أَنذَرَ قَوْمَهُۥ بِٱلْأَحْقَافِ وَقَدْ خَلَتِ ٱلنُّذُرُ مِنۢ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِۦٓ أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوٓا۟ إِلَّا ٱللَّهَ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذَابَ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ (٢١)
And mention [Hud] the brother of ‘Ad, when he warned his people at Ahqaf—and warners have passed away before and after him—saying, ‘Do not worship anyone but Allah. Indeed I fear for you the punishment of a tremendous day.’ (21)
قَالُوٓا۟ أَجِئْتَنَا لِتَأْفِكَنَا عَنْ ءَالِهَتِنَا فَأْتِنَا بِمَا تَعِدُنَآ إِن كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ (٢٢)
They said, ‘Have you come to turn us away from our gods? Then bring us what you threaten us with, if you are truthful. (22)
قَالَ إِنَّمَا ٱلْعِلْمُ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ وَأُبَلِّغُكُم مَّآ أُرْسِلْتُ بِهِۦ وَلَـٰكِنِّىٓ أَرَىٰكُمْ قَوْمًا تَجْهَلُونَ (٢٣)
He said, ‘The knowledge [of when that threat will be carried out] is with Allah alone. I communicate to you what I have been sent with. But I see that you are a senseless lot.’ (23)
فَلَمَّا رَأَوْهُ عَارِضًا مُّسْتَقْبِلَ أَوْدِيَتِهِمْ قَالُوا۟ هَـٰذَا عَارِضٌ مُّمْطِرُنَا ۚ بَلْ هُوَ مَا ٱسْتَعْجَلْتُم بِهِۦ ۖ رِيحٌ فِيهَا عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (٢٤)
When they saw it as a cloud advancing toward their valleys, they said, ‘This cloud brings us rain.’ ‘No, it is what you sought to hasten: a hurricane carrying a painful punishment, (24)
تُدَمِّرُ كُلَّ شَىْءٍۭ بِأَمْرِ رَبِّهَا فَأَصْبَحُوا۟ لَا يُرَىٰٓ إِلَّا مَسَـٰكِنُهُمْ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ (٢٥)
destroying everything by its Lord’s command.’ So they became such that nothing could be seen except their dwellings. Thus do We requite the guilty lot. (25)

Commentary

The People of ‘Ad and the Deadly Gale!

Since the Holy Quran, after mentioning general principles, proceeds to present significant examples to implement those universals, here too, after describing the state of the rebellious and hedonistic arrogant ones, it turns to the story of the people of ‘Ad, which is a clear example of this. It says: "Remind [these polytheists of Makkah] of the story of Hud, the brother of the people of ‘Ad" (وَ اذْكُرْ أَخا عادٍ).

The use of the term "brother" (أخ) is to express the utmost compassion and sincerity of this great Prophet toward his people. As we know, this expression is used in the Holy Quran regarding several great Prophets; they were compassionate and kind brothers to their nations, sparing no sacrifice for them.

This expression may also simultaneously hint at the kinship ties between these Prophets and their peoples.

Then it adds: "[Mention] when he warned his people in the land of 'al-Ahqaf', while many warners [Prophets] had passed away before him in the distant and near past, and after him, warning these nations" (إِذْ أَنْذَرَ قَوْمَهُ بِالْأَحْقافِ وَ قَدْ خَلَتِ النُّذُرُ مِنْ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَ مِنْ خَلْفِهِ).

"Ahqaf," as we have mentioned before, refers to shifting sands that accumulate in deserts due to the blowing of the wind, forming long, winding dunes. From this expression, it becomes clear that the land of the people of ‘Ad was a vast sandy region.

Some have considered it to be in the heart of the Arabian Peninsula between "Najd," "al-Ahsa," "Hadramaut," and "Oman" [^1], but this seems unlikely. This is because other verses of the Quran (in Surah ash-Shu'ara) indicate that the people of ‘Ad lived in a place with abundant water and beautiful trees, and such a thing is very improbable in the heart of the Peninsula.

Another group of commentators has placed it in the southern part of the Peninsula near Yemen, or on the coasts of the Arabian Sea [^2]. Some have also suggested that Ahqaf was a region in the land of Iraq, in the areas of Chaldea and Babylon [^1]. It has been narrated from Tabari that "Ahqaf" is the name of a mountain in Levant (Sham) [^2]. However, considering the linguistic meaning of "Ahqaf" and the fact that their land possessed water and trees while not being immune to shifting sands, the view of those who say this region was in the south of the Arabian Peninsula near the land of Yemen seems closer to the truth.

The phrase "وَ قَدْ خَلَتِ النُّذُرُ مِنْ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَ مِنْ خَلْفِهِ" (and warners had passed away before him and after him [literally: from between his hands and from behind him]) refers to the Prophets who were commissioned before him—some with a short interval, which the Quran describes as "مِنْ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ", and some with a long interval, described as "من خلفه".

As for the possibility suggested by some that this refers to Prophets who came before Hud and after Hud, it is very unlikely, as the phrase "و قد خلت" (and indeed have passed), which denotes the past tense, is not consistent with this.

Now let us see what the content of this great Prophet's call was. The Quran adds: "[He said to them] Worship none but Allah" (أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوا إِلَّا اللَّهَ).

Then he threatened them, saying: "Indeed, I fear for you the punishment of a mighty day" (إِنِّي أَخافُ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذابَ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ).

Although the expression "mighty day" (يوم عظيم) often refers to the Day of Resurrection, it is sometimes applied in Quranic verses to the harsh and terrifying days that passed over previous nations. This is the intended meaning here as well, for in the continuation of these verses we read: "Finally, the people of ‘Ad were seized by divine punishment on a harsh and terrifying day and were blown away."

However, this stubborn and rebellious people stood against this divine call and said to Hud: "Have you come to turn us away from our gods with your lies?" (قالُوا أَ جِئْتَنا لِتَأْفِكَنا عَنْ آلِهَتِنا) [^1] "Then bring us what you threaten us with, if you are of the truthful!" (فَأْتِنا بِما تَعِدُنا إِنْ كُنْتَ مِنَ الصَّادِقِينَ).

These two sentences clearly illustrate the deviation and stubbornness of this rebellious people. In the first sentence, they claim that because his call contradicts the deities they are accustomed to and have inherited from their ancestors, it is a lie and a fabrication.

In the second sentence, they demand the punishment—a punishment from which there is absolutely no return once it occurs. What rational person would wish for such a punishment, even if they were not certain of it?

But Hud, in response to this foolish demand, said: "Knowledge is only with Allah" (قالَ إِنَّمَا الْعِلْمُ عِنْدَ اللَّهِ).

It is He who knows at what time and under what conditions the annihilating punishment shall descend. It is neither related to your demand nor to my desire and will; the goal, which is the completion of the proof (atmam al-hujjah), must be achieved, for His wisdom so requires.

Then he added: "My primary duty is to convey to you that with which I have been sent" (وَ أُبَلِّغُكُمْ ما أُرْسِلْتُ بِهِ).

This is my main responsibility, but the decision regarding obedience to the Lord lies with you, and the will and volition for punishment lie with Him.

"But I see you as a people who persist in ignorance" (وَ لكِنِّي أَراكُمْ قَوْماً تَجْهَلُونَ).

The root of your misery is this very ignorance—an ignorance coupled with stubbornness, pride, and arrogance that does not allow you to study the call of God's messengers. It is an ignorance that compels you to insist on the descent of divine punishment and your own destruction. If you had even a little awareness, you would at least consider that among all the possibilities of denial, there might be one possibility of truth, which, if realized, would leave nothing of you remaining.

Ultimately, the effective advice and brotherly guidance of Hud did not affect those hard-hearted people. Instead of accepting the truth, they grew more stubborn in their false beliefs and persisted, even denying Hud by saying: "If you are truthful, then where is your promised punishment?"

Now that the proof has been sufficiently completed and they have shown their unworthiness to continue living, Divine Wisdom necessitates sending the "annihilating punishment"—the punishment that uproots them.

Suddenly, they observed a cloud appearing on the horizon, rapidly spreading across the sky.

"When they saw it as a cloud advancing toward their valleys, they rejoiced and said: 'This is a cloud bringing us rain!'" (فَلَمَّا رَأَوْهُ عارِضاً مُسْتَقْبِلَ أَوْدِيَتِهِمْ قالُوا هذا عارِضٌ مُمْطِرُنا) [^1]

Commentators have said that rain had not fallen for the people of ‘Ad for some time; the weather had become hot, dry, and suffocating. When the eyes of the people of ‘Ad fell upon the dark, thick, and expansive clouds moving from the distant horizons toward their sky, they were overjoyed. They rushed to welcome it, coming to the edges of the valleys and flood-channels to witness the sight of the descent of blessed rain and to find new life.

But soon they were told: This is not a rain-bearing cloud; "Nay, it is that which you were seeking to hasten" (بَلْ هُوَ مَا اسْتَعْجَلْتُمْ بِهِ).

"A wind wherein is a painful punishment" (رِيحٌ فِيها عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ).

Apparently, the speaker of these words is Allah the Almighty, or perhaps Prophet Hud (peace be upon him) said this to them when he heard their shouts of joy and delight.

Yes, it is a destructive gale that "destroys everything by the command of its Lord" (تُدَمِّرُ كُلَّ شَيْ‌ءٍ بِأَمْرِ رَبِّها). Some commentators have said: "everything" refers to the humans, their livestock, and their property.

For in the next sentence, it adds: "So they became such that nothing could be seen except their dwellings" (فَأَصْبَحُوا لا يُرى‌ إِلَّا مَساكِنُهُمْ).

This indicates that their dwellings remained intact, but they themselves perished, and their bodies and possessions were cast by the gale into distant deserts or into the sea.

Some have said that the first time they realized this black cloud was a dust-filled gale was when it reached near their land. It lifted their livestock and shepherds who were in the surrounding deserts from the ground and carried them into the air; it uprooted tents and lifted them so high they looked like locusts! When they saw this scene, they fled and took refuge in their houses, locking the doors. But the wind tore the doors from their places and smashed the people to the ground (or carried them away), and "Ahqaf"—those very shifting sands—spread over their bodies.

In verse 7 of Surah al-Haqqah, it is stated: "This gale continued for seven nights and eight days." They were constantly groaning under mounds of sand and grit; then the gale carried the sands away, and their bodies became visible once more, and it took them and threw them into the sea! [^1]

In the end, it points to the fact that this fate was not exclusive to this misguided people, but rather: "Thus do We requite the criminal people" (كَذلِكَ نَجْزِي الْقَوْمَ الْمُجْرِمِينَ).

This is a warning to all criminals, sinners, and stubborn, self-centered disbelievers: if you follow this same path, you will have no better fate. Sometimes, the winds which the Quran calls "heralds of His mercy" (مبشرات بَيْنَ يَدَيْ رَحْمَتِهِ)—whose job is to revive dead lands—are given a death-dealing mission.

Sometimes the earth, which is a cradle of peace for man, is turned into his graveyard by a severe earthquake.

And sometimes the rain, which is the source of life for all living beings, is turned into a flood that drowns everything.

Yes, He makes the agents of life into the agents of death. How painful is such a death that arises from the heart of the agent of life? Especially when, like the people of Hud, it creates joy and delight at the beginning so that its punishment may be more agonizing! And interestingly, it says: this wind, these gentle waves of air, by the command of the Lord, crushed everything. [^1]

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ مَكَّنَّـٰهُمْ فِيمَآ إِن مَّكَّنَّـٰكُمْ فِيهِ وَجَعَلْنَا لَهُمْ سَمْعًا وَأَبْصَـٰرًا وَأَفْـِٔدَةً فَمَآ أَغْنَىٰ عَنْهُمْ سَمْعُهُمْ وَلَآ أَبْصَـٰرُهُمْ وَلَآ أَفْـِٔدَتُهُم مِّن شَىْءٍ إِذْ كَانُوا۟ يَجْحَدُونَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ وَحَاقَ بِهِم مَّا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ (٢٦)
Certainly We had granted them power in respects that We have not granted you, and We had vested them with hearing and sight and hearts. But neither their hearing availed them in any way nor did their sight, nor their hearts when they used to impugn the signs of Allah. So they were besieged by what they used to deride. (26)
وَلَقَدْ أَهْلَكْنَا مَا حَوْلَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْقُرَىٰ وَصَرَّفْنَا ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ (٢٧)
Certainly We have destroyed the towns that were around you, and We have variously paraphrased the signs so that they may come back. (27)
فَلَوْلَا نَصَرَهُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ قُرْبَانًا ءَالِهَةًۢ ۖ بَلْ ضَلُّوا۟ عَنْهُمْ ۚ وَذَٰلِكَ إِفْكُهُمْ وَمَا كَانُوا۟ يَفْتَرُونَ (٢٨)
So why did not those [fake deities] help them whom they had taken as gods besides Allah, as a means of nearness [to Him]? Indeed, they forsook them; that was their lie and what they used to fabricate. (28)

Commentary

You Are Never Stronger Than the People of ‘Ad!

These verses are, in fact, a conclusion drawn from the preceding verses which spoke of the painful punishment of the people of ‘Ad.

Addressing the polytheists of Mecca, He says: "We gave the people of ‘Ad a power and position that We have not given to you" (وَ لَقَدْ مَكَّنَّاهُمْ فِيما إِنْ مَكَّنَّاكُمْ فِيهِ‌) [^1]. They were stronger than you in terms of physical strength, and more capable in terms of wealth, property, and material resources. If physical power, wealth, and material civilization could save anyone from the grasp of divine punishment, the people of ‘Ad should not have been tossed around like chaff and straw before a violent wind, leaving nothing behind but their ruined dwellings! This verse is, in fact, similar to what appears in Surah Al-Fajr regarding the same people of ‘Ad: "Have you not seen how your Lord dealt with ‘Ad? [The people of] Iram, [the city] of lofty pillars, the like of which had not been created in the lands" (Surah Al-Fajr 6-8).

Or like what is mentioned in verse 36 of Surah Qaf: "And how many a generation We destroyed before them who were mightier than them in power..."

In short, those stronger than you could not withstand the storm of divine punishment, let alone you.

Then He adds: "We provided them with hearing, sight, and hearts" (وَ جَعَلْنا لَهُمْ سَمْعاً وَ أَبْصاراً وَ أَفْئِدَةً) [^1]. They were also strong and capable in terms of perception, vision, and the discernment of realities; they understood matters well and fully utilized these God-given tools to achieve their material goals.

"But neither their hearing, nor their sight, nor their intellects availed them anything when the divine punishment descended, because they used to reject the signs of Allah" (فَما أَغْنى‌ عَنْهُمْ سَمْعُهُمْ وَ لا أَبْصارُهُمْ وَ لا أَفْئِدَتُهُمْ مِنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ إِذْ كانُوا يَجْحَدُونَ‌ بِآياتِ اللَّهِ) [^2]. And finally, "that which they used to mock encompassed them" (وَ حاقَ بِهِمْ ما كانُوا بِهِ يَسْتَهْزِؤُنَ‌).

Yes, they were equipped with both material means and the means to perceive the truth, but because they approached the divine signs through the path of stubbornness and arrogance, and subjected the words of the prophets to ridicule, the light of truth did not penetrate their hearts.

This very pride, vanity, and enmity toward the truth caused them to be unable to benefit from the means and tools of guidance and cognition—such as sight, hearing, and intellect—to find the path of salvation. Consequently, they were afflicted with the same grim fate mentioned in the previous verses.

When they, with all that power and resources, could achieve nothing, and their lifeless bodies were placed upon the waves of the violent wind like a blade of straw, being tossed in every direction with utter humiliation, what of you, who are weaker and more powerless than them?

It is not difficult for Allah to afflict you as well with the severest punishment for your deeds, and to command the very factors of your life to become the agents of your death and destruction. This is an address to the polytheists of Mecca, and to all arrogant, oppressive, and stubborn human beings throughout all centuries and ages.

Truly, as the Quran says, we are not the first humans to step upon the earth; many other nations lived before us who possessed great resources and power. How good it would be to make a mirror of lessons from their history and behold our own future and destiny within it.

Then, to emphasize this point and provide further admonition, He addresses the polytheists of Mecca, saying: Not only the people of ‘Ad, but "We have already destroyed the rebellious populations that were around you" (وَ لَقَدْ أَهْلَكْنا ما حَوْلَكُمْ مِنَ الْقُرى‌). These were nations whose lands are not far from you and whose dwellings were roughly around the Arabian Peninsula. While the people of ‘Ad lived in the land of "Ahqaf" in the south of the peninsula, the people of "Thamud" lived in a land called "Hijr" in the north of the peninsula, the people of "Saba" with their painful fate lived in the land of "Yemen," the people of "Shu'ayb" lived in the land of "Madyan" on your route toward Sham (Syria), and likewise, the people of "Lot" lived in this same region. All of them were afflicted with various punishments due to their excessive sin, rebellion, and disbelief.

Each of these was a mirror of lessons and an eloquent witness; how is it that despite all these means of awakening, they still do not wake up?

Then He adds: "We have diversified the signs for them in various ways, so that perhaps they might return" (وَ صَرَّفْنَا الْآياتِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ‌).

Sometimes We showed them miracles and extraordinary events; sometimes through blessings, and sometimes through trials and calamities; sometimes by describing the righteous, and sometimes by describing the criminals; and sometimes through the exemplary punishment of others, We gave them admonitions. However, pride, vanity, selfishness, and stubbornness left no room for guidance!

In the last verse under discussion, He reproaches them and condemns them severely with this statement: "Then why did those whom they took as gods besides Allah—as a means of nearness [to Him]—not help them in those difficult and critical moments?" (فَلَوْ لا نَصَرَهُمُ الَّذِينَ اتَّخَذُوا مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ قُرْباناً آلهة) [^1]. Truly, if these deities were rightful, then why did they not help their followers in those critical times and save them from the grasp of terrifying punishments? This itself is a strong proof of the falsehood of their belief, as they imagined these fabricated deities to be a refuge for their day of misfortune.

Then He adds: "Not only did they not help them, but they vanished from them" (بَلْ ضَلُّوا عَنْهُمْ‌).

How can such incompetent and worthless beings—who are the source of no effect, provide no benefit, and disappear during a crisis—be worthy of worship and servitude? And at the end of the verse, He says: "That was their lie and what they used to invent!" (وَ ذلِكَ إِفْكُهُمْ وَ ما كانُوا يَفْتَرُونَ‌).

This destruction and misery, these painful punishments, and this vanishing of the deities at the time of the incident, were the result of their lies, imaginations, and fabrications. [^1]

[^1]: The word "in" (إِنْ) in the phrase "in makkannakum fihi" (إِنْ مَكَّنَّاكُمْ فِيهِ‌) is negative (nafiya), and we have numerous evidences from Quranic verses that support this. However, some have considered it "conditional" (shartiya) or "redundant" (za'ida), which does not seem correct.

[^1]: It is noteworthy that "absar" (eyes) and "af'idah" (hearts and intellects) appear in the plural form, while "sam'" (hearing) is in the singular. This difference may be because "sam'" has an infinitive meaning, and infinitives are always used in the singular, or because of the uniformity of things heard compared to the diversity of things seen and perceived.

[^2]: The word "min" (مِنْ) in "min shay'" (مِنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ) is redundant and for emphasis, meaning it did not benefit them in any way whatsoever.

[^1]: The first object (maf'ul) of "ittakhadhu" (اتخذوا) is omitted, "alihah" (آلهة) is its second object, and "qurbanan" (قرباناً) is a circumstantial qualifier (hal). The implied structure is: "They took them as gods besides Allah while they were [intended as] a means of nearness through them." There is also the possibility that "qurbanan" is an object of purpose (maf'ul la-ajlih). Other possibilities have been suggested for the syntax of the verse which are not significant.

[^1]: Therefore, the verse has an omitted word, and the implied meaning is: "And that was the result of their lie." There is also the possibility that the verse does not require an omission; in that case, the meaning would be: "That was their lie and their fabrications." However, the first meaning seems more appropriate.

Ayahs

وَإِذْ صَرَفْنَآ إِلَيْكَ نَفَرًا مِّنَ ٱلْجِنِّ يَسْتَمِعُونَ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ فَلَمَّا حَضَرُوهُ قَالُوٓا۟ أَنصِتُوا۟ ۖ فَلَمَّا قُضِىَ وَلَّوْا۟ إِلَىٰ قَوْمِهِم مُّنذِرِينَ (٢٩)
When We dispatched toward you a team of jinn listening to the Quran, when they were in its presence, they said, ‘Be silent!’ When it was finished, they went back to their people as warners. (29)
قَالُوا۟ يَـٰقَوْمَنَآ إِنَّا سَمِعْنَا كِتَـٰبًا أُنزِلَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مُوسَىٰ مُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ يَهْدِىٓ إِلَى ٱلْحَقِّ وَإِلَىٰ طَرِيقٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ (٣٠)
They said, ‘O our people! Indeed, we have heard a Book, which has been sent down after Moses, confirming what was before it. It guides to the truth and to a straight path. (30)
يَـٰقَوْمَنَآ أَجِيبُوا۟ دَاعِىَ ٱللَّهِ وَءَامِنُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَغْفِرْ لَكُم مِّن ذُنُوبِكُمْ وَيُجِرْكُم مِّنْ عَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ (٣١)
O our people! Respond to Allah’s summoner and have faith in Him. He will forgive you some of your sins and shelter you from a painful punishment.’ (31)
وَمَن لَّا يُجِبْ دَاعِىَ ٱللَّهِ فَلَيْسَ بِمُعْجِزٍ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَيْسَ لَهُۥ مِن دُونِهِۦٓ أَوْلِيَآءُ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ (٣٢)
Those who do not respond to Allah’s summoner cannot frustrate [Allah] on the earth, and they will not find any protectors besides Him. They are in manifest error. (32)

Occasion of Revelation

Various narrations have been reported regarding the occasion of revelation for these verses, including the following:

The Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله traveled from Mecca toward the Ukaz market in Ta'if, accompanied by Zayd ibn Harithah, with the purpose of inviting the people to Islam. However, no one responded to his call. He was forced to return toward Mecca until he reached a place called the Valley of the Jinn. In the middle of the night, he began reciting the Quran. A group of the Jinn happened to be passing by; when they heard the Prophet’s صلى الله عليه وآله recitation of the Quran, they listened intently and said to one another: "Be silent." When the Prophet finished his recitation, they believed and returned to their people as heralds, inviting them to Islam. A group of them believed and came together to the presence of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله taught them the instructions of Islam. It was then that the above verses and the verses of Surah al-Jinn were revealed. [1]

[1]: Tafsir "Ali ibn Ibrahim" as quoted in "Nur al-Thaqalayn," Vol. 5, p. 19 (with slight summarization).

Others have narrated an occasion of revelation from Ibn Abbas that is similar to the previous one, with the difference being that "the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was engaged in the Fajr (dawn) prayer and was reciting the Quran. A group of Jinn, who were investigating and searching because the cessation of heavenly news had terrified them, heard the sound of the Prophet’s صلى الله عليه وآله recitation and said: 'This is the reason the news of the heavens has been cut off from us.' At that point, they returned to their people and invited them to Islam." [1] The late Tabarsi in "Majma' al-Bayan" has provided a third occasion of revelation here which links the matter to the story of the Prophet’s صلى الله عليه وآله journey to Ta'if, the summary of which is as follows:

"After the death of Abu Talib, matters became difficult for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. He went to Ta'if hoping to find supporters. The nobles of Ta'if vehemently rejected him and pelted the Prophet with stones from behind to such an extent that blood flowed from his blessed feet. Tired and distressed, he came to the edge of a garden and sat in the shade of a date palm while blood was still trickling from his feet.

The garden belonged to Utbah ibn Rabi'ah and Shaybah ibn Rabi'ah, two wealthy men of the Quraysh. The Prophet was saddened by seeing them, as he already knew of their enmity.

The two sent their servant 'Addas, who was a Christian man, with a tray of grapes to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. The Prophet asked 'Addas: 'Where are you from?' He replied: 'From Nineveh!' He asked: 'From the city of the righteous servant of Allah, Yunus (Jonah)?' 'Addas asked: 'How do you know Yunus?' He replied: 'I am the Messenger of Allah; Allah has informed me.' 'Addas realized the truth of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, prostrated for the sake of Allah, and kissed the Prophet’s صلى الله عليه وآله feet.

[1]: This Hadith, which we have summarized, is mentioned in detail in Sahih Bukhari, Sahih Muslim, and Musnad Ahmad (as quoted in "Fi Zilal," Vol. 7, p. 429).

When he returned, Utbah and Shaybah rebuked him, asking why he had done that. He said: 'This is a righteous man who informed me of unknown secrets of the people of this region regarding our Prophet Yunus.' They laughed and said: 'Let him not deceive you from the religion of Christianity, for he is a deceptive man!' The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله returned toward Mecca (while the fruit of this journey was only one believing man) until he reached near a date palm in the middle of the night and began to pray. A group of Jinn from the people of Nasibin or Yemen were passing by; they heard the sound of his Quranic recitation during the Fajr prayer, listened intently, and believed." [1]

Commentary

A Group of Jinn Believe

In these verses—as also indicated in the occasion of revelation—there is a concise discussion regarding the faith of a group of Jinn in the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and His heavenly Book. This is to convey the reality to the polytheists of Mecca: how a seemingly distant group like the Jinn believed in this Prophet who is a human and rose from among you, yet you persist in disbelief and continue your opposition.

Regarding the being called "Jinn" and its characteristics, we will have a detailed discussion in the commentary of Surah al-Jinn, God willing. Here, we will only address the commentary of the verses under discussion:

In reality, the story of the "People of 'Ad" was a warning for the polytheists of Mecca, and the story of the faith of the "Jinn" is another warning.

First, He says: "And [remember] when We directed toward you a group of the jinn, listening to the Qur'an" (وَ إِذْ صَرَفْنا إِلَيْكَ نَفَراً مِنَ الْجِنِّ يَسْتَمِعُونَ الْقُرْآنَ‌).

The expression "صرفنا" (Sarafna) from the root "Sarf," which means transferring something from one state to another, might refer to the fact that the Jinn used to listen to the news of the heavens through "eavesdropping," but with the emergence of the Prophet of Islam, they were turned away from that and directed toward the Quran.

[1]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 9, p. 92—Ibn Hisham has also recorded this story in his history "Al-Sirah al-Nabawiyyah" with slight differences (Vol. 2, pp. 62-63).

"Nafar," as Raghib stated in "Al-Mufradat," means "a group of men who can migrate together." The famous view among linguists is that it refers to a group of three to ten people, and some have said up to forty (though in the Persian language, this term is sometimes applied to a single individual).

Then He adds: "When they attended it, they said, 'Listen in silence!'" (فَلَمَّا حَضَرُوهُ قالُوا أَنْصِتُوا).

This was when the Prophet was reciting the verses of the Quran in the middle of the night or during the Fajr prayer.

"Ansitu" is from the root "Insat," meaning "silence accompanied by listening and attention."

Finally, the light of faith began to shine in their hearts, and they felt the truth of the Quranic verses within their souls; therefore, "when it was concluded, they went back to their people as warners," informing them of the truth they had attained (فَلَمَّا قُضِيَ وَلَّوْا إِلى‌ قَوْمِهِمْ مُنْذِرِينَ‌).

Such is the way of believers: they always seek to inform others of the truths they have realized and to share the sources of their faith with them.

The next verse describes how this group invited their people upon returning to them—a structured, calculated, short, and meaningful invitation: "They said, 'O our people, indeed we have heard a Book revealed after Moses'" (قالُوا يا قَوْمَنا إِنَّا سَمِعْنا كِتاباً أُنْزِلَ مِنْ بَعْدِ مُوسى‌).

This Book has several attributes. First: "confirming what was before it; its content is consistent with their content, and the signs mentioned in previous books are clearly seen in it" (مُصَدِّقاً لِما بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ‌). [1] Another attribute is that "it guides to the truth" (يَهْدِي إِلَى الْحَقِ‌).

In such a way that anyone who employs their intellect and fitrah (innate nature) will clearly find the signs of its truth within it.

And the final attribute: "and to a straight path" (وَ إِلى‌ طَرِيقٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ‌).

The difference between inviting to the "truth" and inviting to the "straight path" apparently lies in the fact that the former refers to true beliefs, while the latter refers to straight and correct practical programs.

The phrase "revealed after Moses" and the phrase "confirming what was before it" support the idea that this group believed in previous heavenly books, especially the Book of Moses, and were seekers of truth.

[1]: We have provided a detailed commentary on this phrase in Volume 1 under verse 41 of Surah al-Baqarah.

If we see that no mention is made of the Book of Jesus (the Gospel) which was revealed after it, it is not because of what Ibn Abbas said—that the Jinn were completely unaware of the revelation of the Gospel—for how could the Jinn be aware of the news of the heavens yet remain so ignorant of the news of the earth?! Rather, it is because the Torah was the primary Book from which even the Christians derived and continue to derive their religious laws.

Then they added: "O our people, respond to the Caller of Allah and believe in Him" (يا قَوْمَنا أَجِيبُوا داعِيَ اللَّهِ وَ آمِنُوا بِهِ‌).

He will grant you two great rewards: "He will forgive you your sins and protect you from a painful punishment" (يَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ مِنْ ذُنُوبِكُمْ وَ يُجِرْكُمْ مِنْ عَذابٍ أَلِيمٍ‌). [1] The "Caller of Allah" refers to the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله who was guiding them toward "Allah." Since man's greatest fear and terror stem from sins and the painful punishment of the Resurrection, they mentioned security against these two matters to attract the most attention.

A group of commentators consider the word "min" (from) in "min dhunubikum" (from your sins) to be "extra" (za'idah), serving as an emphasis on the forgiveness of all sins under the shadow of faith.

However, some consider it "partitive" (tab'idiyyah), referring to those sins committed before embracing faith, or sins related to the "Rights of Allah" (Haqq Allah) rather than the "Rights of People" (Haqq al-Nas).

But it is more appropriate that "min" is extra for emphasis, and the noble verse includes all sins.

In the final verse under discussion, the last words of the Jinn heralds are recounted as follows:

"They said to their people: whoever does not respond to the Caller of Allah cannot escape [Allah's punishment] on earth" (وَ مَنْ لا يُجِبْ داعِيَ اللَّهِ فَلَيْسَ بِمُعْجِزٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ‌).

"And he will have no protectors besides Him" (وَ لَيْسَ لَهُ مِنْ دُونِهِ أَوْلِياءُ).

Therefore, "those are in manifest error" (أُولئِكَ فِي ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ‌).

What error could be worse and more manifest than a person rising in contention against the Truth, the Prophet of God, and even God Himself? For in the entire universe, there is no refuge other than Him, nor can a person flee from the environment of His realm to another place.

We have said many times: "Mu'jiz" (or other derivatives of this word) in such contexts means making [the punisher] unable to pursue or punish, or in other words, escaping from the clutches of punishment.

The expression "on earth" (فِي الْأَرْضِ) indicates that wherever on earth you go, it is the property of Allah and will not be outside the scope of His power. If it does not mention the heavens, it is because the dwelling place of both Jinn and humans is, in any case, on the earth.

Key Points

1- Effective Propagation

As we mentioned, the discussion regarding the Jinn, the nature of their life, and other related characteristics will come, God willing, in the commentary of Surah al-Jinn. What can be derived from the verses under discussion is that they are rational and conscious beings, subject to divine obligations, consisting of both believers and disbelievers, and possessing sufficient awareness of divine calls.

An interesting point in these verses is the method they adopted for propagating Islam among their people.

After attending the presence of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, hearing the verses of the Quran, and realizing its content, they quickly went to their tribe and began preaching to them.

They first spoke of the truth of the Quran and proved this point with three reasons. Then they encouraged them, giving them glad tidings of salvation and deliverance from the clutches of the punishment of the Hereafter under the shadow of faith in this heavenly Book. This was both an emphasis on the issue of "Resurrection" and an attention to the authentic values of the Hereafter compared to the unstable values of the world.

In the third stage, they warned them of the dangers of abandoning faith, providing a warning coupled with reasoning and compassion. Finally, they pointed out the end and consequence of deviating from this path, which is "manifest error."

This method of propagation is effective for any person or group.

2- The Best Proof of the Quran's Greatness is Its Content

From the above verses, as well as from the verses of Surah al-Jinn, it is clearly evident that this group of Jinn was attracted to the Quran solely by hearing its verses. There is no evidence that they requested any other miracle from the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله.

They considered it sufficient for its truth that the Holy Quran is consistent with the signs of previous books, that it invites to the truth, and that its practical planning is upon the straight path.

And indeed, the matter is such that examining the content of the Quran makes us independent of any other proof.

A book presented by an unlettered man in an environment filled with ignorance and superstition, possessing such lofty content, pure knowledge and beliefs, pure monotheism, firm and cohesive laws, strong and powerful arguments, solid and constructive programs, and enlightening and sublime exhortations—all with such powerful attraction and dazzling beauty—is itself the best proof of the truth of this heavenly Book; as they say, "The sun has come as the proof of the sun." [1]

Ayahs

أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَلَمْ يَعْىَ بِخَلْقِهِنَّ بِقَـٰدِرٍ عَلَىٰٓ أَن يُحْـِۧىَ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ ۚ بَلَىٰٓ إِنَّهُۥ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (٣٣)
Do they not see that Allah, who created the heavens and the earth and [who] was not exhausted by their creation, is able to revive the dead? Yes, indeed He has power over all things. (33)
وَيَوْمَ يُعْرَضُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ عَلَى ٱلنَّارِ أَلَيْسَ هَـٰذَا بِٱلْحَقِّ ۖ قَالُوا۟ بَلَىٰ وَرَبِّنَا ۚ قَالَ فَذُوقُوا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَكْفُرُونَ (٣٤)
The day when the faithless are exposed to the Fire, [He will say,] ‘Is this not a fact?’ They will say, ‘Yes, by our Lord!’ He will say, ‘So taste the punishment because of what you used to disbelieve.’ (34)
فَٱصْبِرْ كَمَا صَبَرَ أُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْعَزْمِ مِنَ ٱلرُّسُلِ وَلَا تَسْتَعْجِل لَّهُمْ ۚ كَأَنَّهُمْ يَوْمَ يَرَوْنَ مَا يُوعَدُونَ لَمْ يَلْبَثُوٓا۟ إِلَّا سَاعَةً مِّن نَّهَارٍۭ ۚ بَلَـٰغٌ ۚ فَهَلْ يُهْلَكُ إِلَّا ٱلْقَوْمُ ٱلْفَـٰسِقُونَ (٣٥)
So be patient just as the resolute among the apostles were patient, and do not seek to hasten [the punishment] for them. The day when they see what they are promised, [it will be] as though they had remained [in the world] only an hour of a day. This is a proclamation. So shall anyone be destroyed except the transgressing lot? (35)

Commentary

Be Patient Like the Arch-Prophets (Ulul 'Azm)

These verses, which are the final verses of Surah al-Ahqaf, discuss the "Resurrection," because on one hand, the previous verses quoted from the Jinn preachers made reference to the issue of the "Resurrection."

On the other hand, the early sections of Surah al-Ahqaf spoke of Monotheism (Tawhid), the greatness of the Holy Quran, and the proof of the Prophethood of the Prophet of Islam.

In this final section of the Surah, it brings forward the issue of the "Resurrection," thereby completing the three fundamental principles of belief.

First, He says: "Do they not see that Allah, who created the heavens and the earth and was not fatigued by their creation, is able to give life to the dead? Yes, indeed He is Powerful over all things" (أَ وَ لَمْ يَرَوْا أَنَّ اللَّهَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضَ وَ لَمْ يَعْيَ بِخَلْقِهِنَّ بِقادِرٍ عَلى‌ أَنْ يُحْيِيَ الْمَوْتى‌ بَلى‌ إِنَّهُ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ قَدِيرٌ).

The creation of the heavens and the earth with their various and colorful creatures is a sign of His power over everything, for He has created in this universe everything that can be imagined. With this being the case, how could He be unable to renew the life of human beings? This is a crushing argument regarding the "possibility of the Resurrection."

In principle, the best proof for the possibility of anything is its occurrence. Since we see so much emergence of living and animate beings from inanimate matter, how can we allow doubt into our hearts regarding His absolute power over the Resurrection?

This is one of the numerous proofs for the Resurrection upon which the Holy Quran relies in various verses, including verse 81 of Surah Ya-Sin [1].

In the next verse, it visualizes a scene of the painful punishment of the criminals and deniers of the Resurrection, saying: "And [remember] the Day when those who disbelieved will be exposed to the Fire" (وَ يَوْمَ يُعْرَضُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا عَلَى النَّارِ). Indeed, sometimes Hell is presented to the disbelievers, and sometimes the disbelievers are presented to Hell, and each has a purpose that was mentioned a few verses prior.

When the disbelievers are exposed to the Fire and see its mountain-like, terrifying, and scorching flames, they are asked: "Is this not the truth?" (أَ لَيْسَ هذا بِالْحَقِ‌).

Can you still deny the Resurrection, God's court of justice, and His reward and punishment today, and say these are the superstitious fables of the ancients?!

They, having no choice but to confess, "will say: 'Yes, by our Lord!' [it is the truth]" and there is no room for doubt in it; we were astray when we considered it untrue (قالُوا بَلى‌ وَ رَبِّنا).

[^1]: For more explanation on this subject and the various proofs of the Resurrection, please study the commentary on the final verses of Surah Ya-Sin (Volume 18, pages 456 to 490).

At this moment, Allah or the agents of Divine punishment say: "Then taste the punishment for what you used to deny" (قالَ فَذُوقُوا الْعَذابَ بِما كُنْتُمْ تَكْفُرُونَ‌).

In this way, on that day, they see all realities with their own eyes and confess—a confession and admission that will not benefit them, and will result in nothing but sorrow, regret, the torture of conscience, and spiritual agony.

In the final verse under discussion, which is the last verse of Surah al-Ahqaf, considering what passed in the previous verses regarding the Resurrection and the punishment of the disbelievers, He commands His Prophet: "So be patient, as the Arch-Prophets (Ulul 'Azm) were patient" (فَاصْبِرْ كَما صَبَرَ أُولُوا الْعَزْمِ مِنَ الرُّسُلِ‌).

You are not the only one faced with the opposition and enmity of this people; all the Arch-Prophets faced these difficulties and persevered. "Nuh" عليه السلام, the great Prophet of God, invited people for 950 years, yet only a small group believed in him; they constantly harassed and mocked him.

"Ibrahim" عليه السلام was cast into the fire, and "Musa" عليه السلام was threatened with death, and his heart was filled with blood from the disobedience of his people. And "Isa" the Messiah عليه السلام, after much suffering, they wanted to kill him, but Allah saved him. In short, the world has always been thus, and one cannot triumph over difficulties except through the power of patience and perseverance.

Who Were the Arch-Prophets (Ulul 'Azm)?

There is much discussion among commentators as to who is meant by the "Ulul 'Azm" Prophets. Before we research this, we must analyze the meaning of "Azm," as "Ulul 'Azm" means "possessors of Azm."

"Azm" means a firm and steady will. Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: "Azm means deciding to carry out a matter (binding the heart to the execution of a matter)."

In the Holy Quran, "Azm" is sometimes used in the context of "patience," such as: "And whoever is patient and forgives—indeed, that is of the matters [requiring] determination (Azm)" (Ash-Shura: 43).

And sometimes it means "fulfillment of a covenant," such as: "And We had already taken a promise from Adam before, but he forgot; and We found not in him determination (Azm)" (Ta-Ha: 115).

However, considering that the Prophets who brought a new Sharia (law) and a new religion faced more difficulties and troubles, and required a firmer will and determination to confront them, this group of Prophets has been called "Ulul 'Azm," and the verse under discussion apparently refers to this meaning.

Furthermore, it is an indication that the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله is also among those Prophets, for it says: "You too be patient as the Arch-Prophets were patient."

And if some have interpreted "Azm" and "Azimat" to mean "judgment and Sharia," it is for this reason; otherwise, "Azm" does not linguistically mean Sharia.

In any case, according to this meaning, the word "min" (from) in "min al-rusul" (from the messengers) is "partitive" (tab'idiyyah), referring to a specific group of great Prophets who were possessors of Sharia—the same ones referred to in verse 7 of Surah al-Ahzab: "And [remember, O Muhammad], when We took from the prophets their covenant and from you and from Noah and Abraham and Moses and Jesus, the son of Mary; and We took from them a solemn covenant" (Al-Ahzab: 7).

Here, after mentioning all the Prophets in plural form, He refers to these five great Prophets, which is evidence of their special status.

In verse 13 of Surah ash-Shura, He also speaks of them: "He has ordained for you of religion what He enjoined upon Noah and that which We have revealed to you, [O Muhammad], and what We enjoined upon Abraham and Moses and Jesus..."

Numerous narrations in both Shi'a and Sunni sources have also been cited in this regard, stating that the Ulul 'Azm Prophets were these five individuals. As it appears in a Hadith from Imam al-Baqir and Imam al-Sadiq عليهما السلام:

"There are five of them: the first of them is Nuh, then Ibrahim, then Musa, then Isa, then Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله." [1]

In another Hadith from Imam Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه السلام, it is stated:

"There are five of them, the Arch-Prophets (Ulul 'Azm) from among the Messengers: Nuh, Ibrahim, Musa, Isa, and Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله."

And when the narrator asks: "Why were they named Ulul 'Azm?"

The Imam replies:

"Because they were sent to the East and the West of the earth, and to both Jinn and Mankind." [2]

And again in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام:

"The masters of the Prophets and Messengers are five, and they are the Arch-Prophets (Ulul 'Azm) from among the Messengers, and upon them the millstone [of Prophethood] turns: Nuh, Ibrahim, Musa, Isa, and Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله." [3]

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses discussed (Vol. 9, p. 94). ^2: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 11, p. 58 (Hadith 61). In the same volume, p. 56, Hadith 55 also speaks explicitly on this matter. [^3]: Al-Kafi, Vol. 1, Chapter "Classes of Prophets and Messengers," Hadith 3.

In the commentary Ad-Durr al-Manthur, the same meaning is narrated from Ibn Abbas, that the Ulul 'Azm Prophets are these five. [1]

However, some commentators consider "Ulul 'Azm" to refer to the Prophets who were commanded to fight against enemies. Some have considered their number to be 313, and some consider all Prophets to be Ulul 'Azm (possessors of strong will). According to this latter view, "min" in "min al-rusul" is "explicative" (bayaniyyah) rather than partitive.

However, the first interpretation is the most correct of all, and Islamic narrations support it.

Then the Quran adds following this: "And do not be in haste for them (the disbelievers)" (وَ لا تَسْتَعْجِلْ لَهُمْ‌). For the Resurrection will soon arrive, and they will see with their own eyes what they were in a hurry for; they will be severely punished and realize their mistakes.

The life of this world is so short compared to the Hereafter that "on the Day they see that which they are promised, it will be as though they had not remained [in the world] except for an hour of a day" (كَأَنَّهُمْ يَوْمَ يَرَوْنَ ما يُوعَدُونَ لَمْ يَلْبَثُوا إِلَّا ساعَةً مِنْ نَهارٍ).

[^1]: Ad-Durr al-Manthur, Vol. 6, p. 45.

This feeling of the brevity of worldly life compared to the Hereafter is either because this life is truly no more than an hour compared to that eternal life, or because the world passes so quickly for them that it is as if it were no more than an hour, or because they see the product of their entire life—which they did not utilize correctly—as no more than one hour.

It is here that a flood of regret flows into their hearts, but what use is it when there is no way back?

Thus, we see that when the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was asked about "the distance between this world and the Hereafter," saying: "How much is between the world and the Hereafter?" he replied: "The blink of an eye!" Then he said: Allah has said: "as though they had not remained except for an hour of a day." [1] This shows that the expression "hour" (sa'ah) does not mean the literal measure of a standard hour, but refers to a small and short amount of time.

Then, as a warning to all human beings, He adds: "[This is] a notification (balagh)" (بلاغ) [2]—for all those who have strayed from the line of servitude to the Lord, for those who are drowned in the fleeting life of the world and its desires, and finally, it is a notification for all inhabitants of this unstable world.

And in the final sentence, within a meaningful and threatening interrogation, He says: "Will any be destroyed except the defiant people?" (فَهَلْ يُهْلَكُ إِلَّا الْقَوْمُ الْفاسِقُونَ‌).

Key Point: The Prophet of Islam was the Legend of Patience and Perseverance

The lives of the great Prophets of God, especially the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, demonstrate their boundless resistance against harsh events, severe storms, and grueling difficulties. Given that the path of truth always contains such difficulties, the travelers of the path of truth must take inspiration from them in this divine journey.

We usually look back at the dark days of the past from the bright point of Islamic history, and this perspective—from "future to past"—visualizes realities differently. We must imagine ourselves in those days when the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was all alone, with no sign of victory visible on the horizon of his life.

Obstinate enemies were determined to destroy him, and even his close relatives were in the front line of this struggle! He would constantly go among the Arab tribes and invite them, but no one would respond to his call.

They would stone him such that blood flowed from his blessed body, yet he would not abandon his program.

They placed him under such social, economic, and political siege that all paths were closed to him and his few followers; some perished from hunger, and some were brought down by illness.

They tortured his few companions—tortures that weighed heavily upon his soul and heart.

Days passed for the Prophet that are difficult to describe with words or pen. When he went to "Ta'if" to invite the people to Islam, not only did they not accept his invitation, but they threw so many stones at him that blood flowed from his feet.

They incited the ignorant to shout and insult him. He was forced to take refuge in a garden, sat in the shade of a tree, and whispered to his God thus:

"O Allah! To Thee I complain of my weakness, my lack of resources, and my humiliation before the people. O Most Merciful of the merciful! Thou art the Lord of the oppressed, and Thou art my Lord. To whom dost Thou entrust me? To a stranger who treats me with hostility? Or to an enemy to whom Thou hast given mastery over my affair? If Thou art not angry with me, then I do not care..." [1]

Sometimes they called him a sorcerer, and sometimes they addressed him as a madman.

Sometimes they poured ashes on his head, and sometimes they resolved to kill him and surrounded his house with swords.

Yet, despite all these conditions, he continued with patience, endurance, and perseverance.

And finally, he tasted the sweet fruit of this tree; his religion encompassed not only the Arabian Peninsula but the East and West of the world. Today, the call of the Adhan, which is the cry of his victory, is heard every morning and evening from the four corners of the earth and across all five continents.

And this is the meaning of: "So be patient, as the Arch-Prophets were patient."

[^1]: Rawdat al-Wa'izin, as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 25.

[^3]: Sirah Ibn Hisham, Vol. 2, p. 61.

And this is the way and method of struggling against devils and demons, and the path to victory over them and attaining great Divine goals.

With this being the case, how can those who seek ease expect to reach their great goals without patience and enduring pain and suffering?

How can the Muslims of today expect to be victorious against all these enemies who are determined to destroy them, without taking inspiration from the original school of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله?

Especially the Islamic leaders are commanded to follow this program more than anyone else, as it appears in a Hadith from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام:

"Patience and perseverance are obligatory upon the leaders of the affairs, because Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said to His Prophet: 'So be patient, as the Arch-Prophets were patient,' and He made the like of that obligatory upon His friends and the people of His obedience by His saying: 'There has certainly been for you in the Messenger of Allah an excellent example' (and you all must follow him)." [1]

O Allah! Grant us this great bounty, this heavenly gift—this patience, endurance, and perseverance against difficulties! O Lord! Give us the success to keep this lamp of guidance—which Your Arch-Prophets, especially the Seal of the Prophets, Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله, placed before humanity with grueling efforts—ever burning and to guard it worthily.

[^1]: Ihtijaj al-Tabarsi, as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 23.

O God! We know that the enemies of truth are organized and united, and they refrain from no crime. Grant us patience and endurance more than what they have in power, so that we never kneel before the multitude of difficulties, and that we may leave the waves and storms behind—and this is not possible except through Your endless aid and grace. Amen, O Lord of the Worlds.

End of Surah al-Ahqaf. Friday, 3rd of Ramadan, 1405 (May 24, 1985).
